channel.title: Economy on Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar
channel.description: Recent content in Economy on Shit PH Said, Mostly By Anwar

item.title: Cabinet Maintains RON95 at RM1.99 — Anwar Calls It a 'Difficult Decision' While Dodging Subsidy Reform for the Third Year Running
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced on March 11, 2026 — following a Cabinet meeting — that the price of RON95 petrol under the Budi95 targeted subsidy mechanism will remain at RM1.99 per litre. He called it a “difficult decision,” noting that the market price of RON95 has risen roughly threefold due to the escalating US-Iran conflict disrupting oil markets and threatening Gulf shipping routes. RON97 had already risen to RM3.25, unsubsidised RON95 to RM2.67, and diesel to RM3.12. Anwar framed the decision as a sacrifice for the rakyat.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not generosity — it is the collapse of reform. Anwar’s government promised targeted fuel subsidy restructuring as a cornerstone of fiscal discipline. The Budi95 mechanism, launched as a watered-down compromise, was supposed to be the first step toward removing blanket subsidies. Instead, a geopolitical crisis has given the government the perfect excuse to shelve reform indefinitely while spending billions on untargeted fuel subsidies ahead of widely expected snap elections. Every week the subsidy is maintained at current market spreads, Malaysia hemorrhages fiscal resources that could go to healthcare, education, or infrastructure.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar has been “about to reform” fuel subsidies since he took office in November 2022. In 2023, it was “next year.” In 2024, it was “soon.” In 2025, the Budi95 mechanism finally launched — then immediately became a political tool rather than a fiscal one. Just five days ago, Anwar admitted he could only hold the RM1.99 price for “one or two months.” Now, with Hari Raya approaching and elections rumoured for mid-2026, the Cabinet “decides” to maintain it — as if there was ever a chance they’d raise fuel prices weeks before a festive season and a potential snap poll. This isn’t a difficult decision. It’s the only decision a government terrified of voters would make.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has been trapped in the fuel subsidy cycle for decades. Najib’s government removed RON95 subsidies in 2014 with a managed float, only for Mahathir’s PH 1.0 government to reintroduce price ceilings. Anwar promised to break the pattern with targeted subsidies — means-tested, fiscally responsible, reform-driven. Instead, Budi95 kept the same RM1.99 price for the vast majority of motorists and excluded only foreign-registered vehicles and luxury cars. The IMF, World Bank, and Malaysia’s own fiscal policy advisors have repeatedly warned that blanket fuel subsidies are regressive, benefiting the wealthy more than the poor. Anwar knows this. He cited these studies himself when he was in opposition.

🎯 PH Callout

You told Malaysians you’d be different. You said you’d make the hard calls Najib and Muhyiddin wouldn’t. You quoted fiscal responsibility in every speech, promised targeted subsidies in every manifesto, and lectured the opposition about populism from every podium. Now you’re spending billions to keep fuel cheap during Ramadan, ahead of Hari Raya, ahead of elections you’re quietly preparing for. This isn’t reform. This is the oldest trick in Malaysian politics — and you swore you’d never play it. Every ringgit spent on untargeted subsidies is a ringgit stolen from the structural reforms you promised the rakyat.

Sources: The Star — Cabinet decides to maintain subsidised RON95 price at RM1.99 | Malay Mail | The Star — PM: Fuel subsidy can last up to two months (March 7) | paultan.org — Budi95 petrol price maintained (March 6)

item.title: Oil Past $100, Strait of Hormuz Shut, Rakyat Burning Fuel to Commute — Anwar Won't Mandate WFH, Freeze Tolls, Cap Food Prices, or Cut GLC Fat
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-11

📌 What Happened

The Iran-US war has pushed global oil past $100 per barrel, effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz — through which nearly 80% of Asia’s oil transits — and sent Malaysia’s FBM KLCI plunging 2.55% in a single session on March 9. RON97 is up to RM3.25, diesel to RM3.12, and Anwar himself admitted on March 7 that the government can only hold RON95 at RM1.99 for ‘one or two more months.’ Freight costs are surging, food prices are next, and experts from NST to Hong Leong IB are warning of imported inflation hitting the rakyat’s kitchen table. Anwar’s response so far: a strongly worded tweet about solidarity, an emergency parliamentary motion condemning Israel, and Zafrul saying Malaysia is ‘prepared’ — without naming a single concrete domestic measure to protect workers and families from the economic fallout.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is not a theoretical crisis. Malaysians are already paying more at the pump for RON97 and diesel. Freight costs feed directly into food, medicine, and essential goods. The stock market is bleeding. And yet the Prime Minister — who is also the Finance Minister — has not announced a single emergency domestic economic measure. No crisis-mode intervention. No relief. Just speeches about geopolitics while the rakyat’s cost of living climbs in real time. Malaysia proved during COVID that the government can act decisively on domestic economic protection when it wants to. The difference is political will — and right now, Anwar’s will is directed at Parliament speeches about Zionist plots, not at shielding the people he governs.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Four things Anwar could do tomorrow with existing executive power — and won’t:

  1. Mandate work-from-home for all WFH-compatible jobs. Malaysia already has a Flexible Work Arrangement framework under the Employment Act. During COVID, the government mandated WFH and it worked. With oil past $100 and commuters burning RM300-500/month on fuel and tolls, a temporary WFH directive for office-based workers would immediately cut household fuel costs, reduce demand on subsidised petrol, and ease the fiscal pressure Anwar says he can only hold for two months. Instead, millions of KL workers sit in traffic burning fuel the government can barely afford to subsidise.

  2. Freeze or waive highway tolls during the crisis. The government already broke its toll abolition promise — paying RM591 million just to freeze hikes rather than remove tolls. A temporary toll waiver during a war-driven energy crisis is basic economic relief. Singapore waived ERP during COVID disruptions. Malaysia won’t even pause toll collection while fuel prices spike.

  3. Impose emergency price controls on essential food items. Freight costs from Hormuz disruptions are already feeding into import prices. Cooking oil, flour, rice, and protein prices will follow. The Price Control and Anti-Profiteering Act 2011 gives the government power to gazette maximum prices for essential goods. Anwar used this power selectively before. Now, when a global war is about to hit Malaysian dinner tables, silence.

  4. Freeze non-essential GLC and political appointment spending. The IDEAS report found 238 political appointments to FSBs and GLCs under Anwar. Every one of those comes with salaries, allowances, and perks funded by public money. During a war-driven fiscal crunch — with the subsidy bill ballooning and oil revenue uncertain — a responsible government would freeze all discretionary patronage spending and redirect it to rakyat relief. Instead, the appointments continue.

📜 Previous Cases

During COVID-19, the Muhyiddin government — for all its democratic illegitimacy — implemented a blanket WFH directive, moratoriums on loan repayments, cash handouts (BPN), and wage subsidies within weeks of the crisis hitting. The Ismail Sabri government continued with hybrid WFH arrangements and extended moratoriums. These were imperfect governments acting under crisis pressure. Anwar, who controls both the PM’s office and the Finance Ministry, has more concentrated fiscal power than either predecessor — and has used less of it in response to a war that is already hitting Malaysian wallets harder than the early weeks of COVID hit fuel prices. The precedent exists. The tools exist. The power exists. The will does not.

🎯 PH Callout

You told us you were different. You told us Madani meant the people come first. You told us having the PM also serve as Finance Minister would mean faster, more decisive economic management. So where is it? Oil is past $100. The Strait of Hormuz is disrupted. Your own deputy economy minister admits growth projections are shaky. Your own mouth admitted the RON95 subsidy has weeks left. And your crisis response is… a parliamentary motion about Iran and a tweet about solidarity? Mandate WFH. Freeze tolls. Cap food prices. Cut the GLC fat. You have the power. You have the precedent. You have the tools. Use them — or admit that ‘Ekonomi Madani’ was never about protecting the rakyat. It was about protecting the coalition.

Sources: Xinhua — KLCI plunges 2.55% | SAYS — Oil spike impact on Malaysia | The Star — RON95 subsidy 1-2 months | NST — Oil price surge could hit Malaysia | CNBC — Strait of Hormuz impact | Straits Times — Malaysia subsidy bill pressure | Malaysiakini — 238 political appointments | Asia Media Centre — Hormuz buffer

item.title: Anwar Announces RM500 Cash Handout for Civil Servants and Mosque Staff — Hari Raya Election Season Begins
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 9 March 2026, Anwar Ibrahim announced Special Financial Assistance (Bantuan Kewangan Khas, BKK) 2026: RM500 for civil servants Grade 15 and below, RM250 for pensioners and veterans, and RM500 for approximately 70,000 mosque and religious personnel — imams, bilal, siak, marbut, KAFA and takmir teachers. Payments land on 13 March, days before Hari Raya Aidilfitri, when household spending peaks and political goodwill is most visible. Anwar holds both the PM and Finance Minister portfolios, giving him unilateral authority over the timing and targeting of such disbursements.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: This is textbook election-cycle spending: targeted, populist, and timed for maximum emotional impact during a religious holiday. Bundling civil servants and mosque personnel in the same announcement shores up two critical vote banks simultaneously — the bureaucracy and the religious establishment. The RM500 for mosque staff is a transparent attempt to undercut PAS’s monopoly on the religious vote, funded not by institutional reform but by direct cash transfers from public funds. When a PM who is also Finance Minister announces pre-Raya handouts with no independent fiscal check, the line between governance and campaigning disappears entirely.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Cash handouts before festive seasons is the BN playbook that PH spent decades criticising. Najib’s BR1M was mocked as “vote buying” by PH in opposition. Anwar’s BKK is the same thing with a different name. The inclusion of mosque personnel is particularly cynical: these roles are traditionally associated with PAS-aligned rural and semi-urban constituencies, making this a direct bid to compete with PAS on religious ground using public money. If you wanted to improve the lives of mosque workers, you’d reform their employment terms and pay scales permanently — not throw them a pre-Raya bonus when you need their votes.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Najib’s BR1M (later BSH) was the original festive cash handout programme, widely criticised by PH as populist vote-buying. Muhyiddin’s BPN during COVID was similarly timed for political impact. Every Malaysian PM discovers the electoral power of cash transfers before major holidays. Anwar promised to be different. He isn’t.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You mocked BR1M for years. Called it bribery. Called it unsustainable. Now you’re doing the exact same thing, timed to the exact same calendar, targeting the exact same vote banks — with the added bonus of buying the mosque vote with taxpayer money. If this is what ‘Malaysia Madani’ looks like, it’s just 1Malaysia with better branding.

Sources: Malay Mail

item.title: Anwar Brings Forward RM15 Billion STR Cash Handout — Record Spending, Hari Raya Timing, Snap Election Vibes
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On March 9, 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced that Phase 2 of the Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR) cash transfer programme would be brought forward to March 10 — weeks ahead of its originally scheduled date. The early disbursement will benefit 5.2 million recipients.

Anwar framed the move as helping Malaysians prepare for Hari Raya Aidilfitri. The total 2026 allocation for STR and SARA (Sumbangan Asas Rahmah) stands at RM15 billion — the highest in the history of the Federal Government’s direct cash assistance programmes.

This comes on the same day Anwar also announced a separate RM500 special financial aid for civil servants in Grade 15 and below (to be paid March 13) and mosque staff.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Three things make this significant:

  1. Record spending on cash transfers. RM15 billion is not pocket change. This is the largest direct cash handout allocation any Malaysian government has ever made — and it comes from a PM who campaigned on fiscal discipline, subsidy rationalisation, and ending the culture of handouts.
  2. The timing is suspicious. Reuters reported on March 6 that government sources say Anwar could call snap elections as early as July 2026. Bringing forward cash payments right before Hari Raya — the single most politically sensitive festive period in Malaysia — is textbook pre-election cash politics.
  3. Structural reform is nowhere. Cash transfers are not economic policy. They are a painkiller. The RON95 fuel subsidy rationalisation has been delayed repeatedly. The promised targeted subsidy mechanism is still not operational. Instead of fixing the system, the government is writing bigger cheques.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Anwar spent years — decades — attacking BN’s BR1M cash handout programme as vote-buying, populist bribery, and fiscal irresponsibility. He called it a “crutch” that kept the rakyat dependent on government generosity instead of building real economic resilience.

Now he’s running the exact same playbook, except he’s spending more money and bringing payments forward to land right before Hari Raya. STR is BR1M with a rebrand. The only difference is the name on the cheque.

The “highest allocation in history” line is not a flex — it’s a confession. If the economy is performing as well as the government claims (4-4.5% GDP growth, record approved investments), why does the government need to spend a record RM15 billion on cash handouts? Either the growth isn’t reaching ordinary people, or this is about elections, not economics. Probably both.

Anwar urged recipients to “spend wisely.” Here’s a thought: maybe the government should spend wisely too.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

  • BR1M under Najib (2012-2018): Anwar and PH relentlessly attacked Barisan Nasional’s Bantuan Rakyat 1Malaysia (BR1M) as unsustainable vote-buying. DAP called it “irresponsible fiscal policy.” PKR called it “bribing the poor.” Now they run a bigger version of the same programme.
  • 2024 Hari Raya handouts: Anwar brought forward STR payments before Hari Raya 2024 as well. This is now an annual pattern — accelerate cash disbursements to coincide with the festive season for maximum political goodwill.
  • RON95 subsidy delays (2024-2026): The government has repeatedly postponed targeted fuel subsidy reforms. Instead of fixing the subsidy structure, they keep layering cash transfers on top. The fiscal consolidation story — deficit down from 5.5% to 3.5% — looks less impressive when you’re simultaneously running record cash transfer programmes.
  • Snap election signals (March 2026): Reuters reported on March 6 that two government lawmakers confirmed Anwar could call snap polls as early as July. Cash before elections is the oldest trick in Malaysian politics.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You promised “reformasi, bukan derma” — reform, not charity. You told Malaysians that PH would build an economy where people didn’t need handouts. You mocked Najib for BR1M. You called it a plaster on a broken bone.

Now you’re spending RM15 billion on the same plaster — and you’re proud of it. You brought the payment forward to land right before Raya, right before the rumoured snap election window. And you have the audacity to call this “Rahmah” — compassion.

This isn’t compassion. This is campaign spending filed under a different budget line. If you actually believed in reform, you’d be fixing wages, fixing subsidies, and fixing the structural reasons people need these handouts in the first place. Instead, you’re writing record cheques and hoping the rakyat forgets you promised something better.

Sources: Bernama | Malay Mail | The Star | FMT | The Edge | Reuters (snap election signals)

item.title: Anwar Launches Capital Market Masterplan, Says 'Reform Non-Negotiable' — While Every Actual Reform Gets Delayed or Killed
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim delivered the keynote address at the launch of the Securities Commission’s Capital Market Masterplan 2026–2030 (CMP4) on March 9, 2026. He declared that “the government has made reform non-negotiable” and that Malaysia’s capital market “must drive shared prosperity, not just profit.”

The CMP4 targets growing Malaysia’s capital market to RM6.3 trillion by 2030 and includes initiatives like the “MY Value Up” program to boost PLC valuations.

Also present were Second Finance Minister Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan and SC Chairman Datuk Mohammad Faiz Azmi.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

The phrase “reform non-negotiable” would be powerful if it came from a government that was actually delivering reform. Instead, it comes from the same Prime Minister whose government, in the past two weeks alone:

  • Failed to pass the PM tenure limit bill because 8 of its own MPs didn’t show up (March 3)
  • Postponed the AG-Public Prosecutor separation bill for a 3-month committee review — a core reform promise delayed yet again (March 4)
  • Refused to suspend MACC chief Azam Baki despite mounting allegations, opting instead to let his contract quietly expire in May (reported March 9)
  • Delayed RON95 reform repeatedly — Anwar himself admitted he could only hold the price “one or two more months” (March 7) “Reform non-negotiable” is a slogan delivered at a securities industry event while actual institutional reform — the kind that strengthens governance and accountability — keeps getting pushed back, watered down, or abandoned.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Anwar uses “reform” the way a luxury brand uses “exclusive” — it is a marketing word detached from the product.

The Capital Market Masterplan is a Securities Commission document. It is a technocratic roadmap for market growth, IPO pipelines, and PLC valuations. That is fine. But Anwar does not get to stand at a podium, declare “reform non-negotiable,” and receive credit for reform when:

  • The Attorney General still doubles as the Public Prosecutor — a conflict of interest PH has promised to fix since 2018.
  • The MACC chief remains in office despite allegations that would have triggered immediate suspension in any serious anti-corruption jurisdiction.
  • Institutional reforms that require political courage — not just SC planning documents — are systematically delayed. Capital market growth is not reform. Raising PLC valuations is not reform. “Shared prosperity” is not reform. Reform is when you change the rules that protect the powerful. Anwar has not done that.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

  • 2018–2020 (PH 1.0): The first PH government also promised AG-PP separation, IPCMC, and institutional reform. None were completed before the Sheraton Move collapsed the government.
  • March 4, 2026: Fahmi Fadzil announced the AG-PP separation bill would be delayed for a 3-month parliamentary committee review — the latest in a long line of postponements.
  • March 3, 2026: The PM tenure limit constitutional amendment failed because PH couldn’t muster its own full attendance — 8 government and independent MPs were absent.
  • December 2025: Anwar halfed Najib’s sentence while the 1MDB debt — RM50 billion — remained on the rakyat’s books.
  • The pattern is consistent: PH announces bold reform frameworks at formal events, then delays or abandons the actual legislative and institutional changes required to implement them.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You say reform is “non-negotiable.” Then why does every reform end up being negotiated away?

The AG-PP separation — delayed. The PM tenure limit — failed on your own attendance. Azam Baki — still in office. RON95 — still subsidised with no timeline. IPCMC — abandoned.

You have the parliamentary majority. You have the institutional mandate. You have the public goodwill — or what remains of it.

What you don’t have is the political will to spend your coalition capital on anything that doesn’t benefit the coalition itself.

Stop telling the Securities Commission audience that reform is non-negotiable. Start telling your own Cabinet.

Sources: The Star | NST | The Star (CMP4 keynote) | The Edge | Bernama

item.title: Anwar's Government Fights 531,976 Retirees in Court — Wins Appeal to Block RM1.7 Billion Pension Payout
item.description:
Said by: Federal Government / Senior Federal Counsel Sallehuddin Ali

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 What Happened

On 9 March 2026, the Court of Appeal unanimously overturned a High Court order that would have required Anwar Ibrahim’s government to pay RM1.7 billion in pension arrears to 531,976 former civil servants. The three-judge panel — led by Justice Azhahari Kamal Ramli, with Justices Lim Chong Fong and Meor Hashimi Abdul Hamid — ruled that the pensioners’ application was res judicata (already adjudicated) and constituted an “abuse of court process.” The government, represented by Senior Federal Counsel Sallehuddin Ali, successfully argued that the 2016 Public Service Department Circular (PP 1/2016) was an “enhancement” to the civil service scheme, not a mandatory salary revision triggering pension adjustments. The original case was brought by Aminah Ahmad, a retired Ministry of Foreign Affairs officer, on behalf of 56 other retirees in 2017.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the Madani government actively going to court to avoid paying half a million retirees money they were legally awarded. The average payout per retiree would have been roughly RM3,195 — not a fortune, but real money for people on fixed incomes. The government chose to fight this rather than honour a High Court order. This is a government that spends billions on Hari Raya cash handouts and election-year STR payments, but when pensioners win a court ruling for pension adjustments based on the government’s own 2016 circular, Putrajaya deploys federal counsel to crush the claim on a technicality. The signal is clear: if you’re politically useful, you get cash. If you’re a retiree with a legal claim, you get lawyers.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar Ibrahim built his career on defending the little guy. His government now argues that a PSD circular meant to adjust pensions in line with civil service salary changes was merely an “enhancement” — bureaucratic doublespeak for “we wrote it, but we didn’t mean it.” The Court of Appeal’s ruling that this was “abuse of process” doesn’t mean the pensioners’ grievance is illegitimate — it means the government found a procedural exit ramp and took it. Meanwhile, Putrajaya approved RM15 billion in STR cash handouts just days earlier. The message: Anwar’s government has money for voters, not for retirees who already served the country.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysian governments have a long history of treating pension adjustments as political favours rather than legal obligations. The 2013 amendment to the Pension Adjustment Act — which provided retirees with a fixed annual 2% increase — was itself invalidated by the Court of Appeal in 2022, stripping retirees of a guaranteed mechanism. Every administration since has treated pension reform as a hot potato: promise it during campaigns, dodge it in office. The Madani government is no different. Anwar’s own 2022 election manifesto spoke of “dignifying” civil servants and retirees, but the legal strategy his government is pursuing tells a different story.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised kerajaan Madani — a civilised government that upholds justice and human dignity. Half a million people who spent their careers serving this country were told by a High Court that they are owed pension adjustments. Your government’s response was not to pay, not to negotiate, but to appeal and win on a technicality. You found RM15 billion for pre-Raya handouts but fought tooth and nail against RM1.7 billion for pensioners. That is not reform. That is not Madani. That is the same old Putrajaya arithmetic: spend where the votes are, litigate where they aren’t.

Sources: NST | Malay Mail | The Star | Bernama | SAYS | The Edge

item.title: Deputy Economy Minister Says Growth Steady at 4-4.5% — Amid a Regional War and Global Volatility
item.description:
Said by: Mohd Shahar Abdullah, Deputy Economy Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 9 March 2026, Deputy Economy Minister Mohd Shahar Abdullah projected 4–4.5% GDP growth for Malaysia at the Asean Banking and Finance Summit, citing domestic consumption, infrastructure investment, the digital economy, and Visit Malaysia 2026. He made this statement at the very summit whose chairman, Tan Sri Majid Khan, warned that geopolitical tensions are “repricing risk across our entire region.” The US-Iran conflict was escalating, oil prices were spiking due to Strait of Hormuz disruptions, and GPS jamming was affecting shipping across the Gulf.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A deputy minister projecting steady growth at a summit convened to discuss regional instability suggests a dangerous disconnect between political messaging and economic reality. The growth drivers cited — tourism, digital economy, domestic consumption — are all vulnerable to the very geopolitical risks flagged at the same event. Oil price spikes may temporarily boost petroleum revenue, but they raise input costs across the economy and threaten the government’s already fragile fuel subsidy position. When your own summit chairman is sounding the alarm, doubling down on optimism reads less like confidence and more like denial.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Malaysia’s economy is heavily trade-dependent and exposed to every global shock the deputy minister chose to ignore. Projecting 4-4.5% growth while a regional war threatens shipping lanes, oil markets, and investor confidence is not leadership — it’s PR. The government has repeatedly been caught off-guard by external shocks (COVID, supply chain disruptions, commodity swings) because it prioritises messaging over contingency planning. Telling bankers “everything is fine” while the building shakes is not reassuring — it’s alarming.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysian governments have a long history of projecting optimism that doesn’t survive contact with reality. In 2020, the government insisted the economy was “resilient” weeks before COVID lockdowns crashed GDP. In 2023, growth projections were revised downward multiple times. The pattern: announce best-case projections, hope the world cooperates, scramble when it doesn’t. PH inherited this habit from BN and has done nothing to change it.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: If you’re going to host a summit about regional risk, don’t send your deputy minister to say everything is fine. Either acknowledge the risks and present contingency plans, or stop pretending your growth projections are anything more than political targets. Malaysians deserve honest economic forecasting, not election-season optimism disguised as policy analysis.

Sources: The Edge Malaysia

item.title: It's March 2026 — Where Is Anwar's Promise to Eradicate Hardcore Poverty by Q1?
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 7 December 2025, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim publicly declared that Malaysia would eradicate hardcore poverty by the first quarter of 2026. Speaking at the closing of the Rancakkan Madani programme in Putrajaya, Anwar cited a hardcore poverty rate of 0.09% — roughly 7,000 households — and said the remaining cases could be resolved within months. “We are one of the countries in the world… we have managed to reduce the national rate of hardcore poverty to just 0.09 per cent,” Anwar said. “We can end it by the first quarter of 2026.” The promise was reported by Bernama, Malay Mail, The Star, New Straits Times, The Edge, and The Sun. It was a flagship claim — repeated during Budget 2026 and the Rancakkan Madani roadshow. It is now 9 March 2026. Q1 ends in three weeks. There has been no official announcement that hardcore poverty has been eradicated. No press conference. No Bernama flash. No triumphant PMO statement.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: This was not a casual remark. Anwar made the eradication of hardcore poverty a centrepiece of the Madani economic narrative. It was embedded into Budget 2026 messaging, cited in international forums, and used to distinguish Malaysia from Western economies plagued by homelessness. The World Bank even weighed in, commending Malaysia’s progress while noting that “further improvements in education and health outcomes are crucial.” The December 2025 promise was designed to be a legacy moment — the PM who ended extreme poverty. If the target has been met, the silence is bizarre. If it hasn’t been met, the silence is damning. Either way, the rakyat deserves an update. Poverty eradication claims also carry methodological risk. Economists have previously questioned whether Malaysia’s hardcore poverty line (RM1,169/month per household) is set too low, making “eradication” a statistical exercise rather than a lived reality. A government that declares victory without addressing these concerns is doing PR, not policy.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: This is a textbook case of promise inflation followed by quiet deflation. Anwar made the pledge when the numbers looked good and the cameras were rolling. He set a specific, time-bound target — Q1 2026 — knowing full well it would either be met or missed in public view. Now that the deadline is here, the government has gone quiet. No update. No revised timeline. No acknowledgement that the target even exists. This is the same administration that loudly claims credit for every ringgit of GDP growth and every decimal point drop in inflation. But when it comes to a concrete promise with a concrete deadline? Silence. If hardcore poverty has been eradicated, say so. Publish the data. Show the methodology. Let independent economists verify it. If it hasn’t been eradicated, say that too. Explain what went wrong. Revise the timeline. Be honest. What you don’t get to do is make a grand promise, ride the PR wave, and then pretend the deadline doesn’t exist.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: This is not PH’s first vanishing deadline:

  • RON95 fuel subsidy reform has been delayed repeatedly. Anwar himself admitted in early March 2026 that the government could only hold the price for “one or two more months” — yet no concrete reform plan has been announced.
  • The AG-Public Prosecutor separation bill was postponed in March 2026 for a 3-month committee review, after being a signature reform promise.
  • The PM term limit constitutional amendment failed in Parliament in March 2026 — despite PH’s commanding majority — because 8 government MPs didn’t even show up to vote. The pattern is clear: big promises, loud announcements, quiet retreats. The Madani government treats reform pledges as PR events, not binding commitments. Malaysia’s own history is littered with poverty eradication claims that didn’t survive contact with reality. The Najib administration claimed similar progress; independent audits later showed significant undercounting.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You told 33 million Malaysians that hardcore poverty would be gone by Q1 2026. You said it on camera, in Putrajaya, at your own showcase event. Bernama carried it. Every major outlet reported it. It’s March 9. Q1 is almost over. Where’s the announcement? Where’s the data? Where’s the press conference where you pop the champagne and show us the zero? Or did you hope we’d forget? Here’s the thing about specific promises with specific deadlines: people remember. You don’t get to coast on vibes when you gave us a date. Either deliver the results or deliver an explanation. The rakyat who are still living on less than RM1,169 a month deserve more than silence.

Sources: Bernama | Malay Mail | The Star | The Edge | The Sun | NST

item.title: Zafrul Says Malaysia 'Prepared' for Middle East Conflict Economic Fallout — More Vibes, No Plan
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, Senior Political Advisor to PM

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-09

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 9 March 2026, Tengku Zafrul — now Senior Political Advisor to the PM — told media that the government is ‘prepared to face any eventuality affecting the country’s economic position’ from the Middle East conflict, including implications for petrol subsidies. He noted oil had previously reached US$100/barrel and said the Finance Ministry and Economy Ministry ‘have made preparations.’ He disclosed no specific preparations.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The US-Iran conflict is escalating, oil prices are spiking, GPS jamming is disrupting Gulf shipping, and Malaysia’s economy is directly exposed through energy imports and trade routes. ‘We’re monitoring’ and ‘we’re prepared’ are not policies — they’re press release filler. The government has not disclosed what fiscal buffers exist, what the trigger point for RON95 subsidy reform would be, or what contingency plans exist for a sustained oil price shock.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: This is the same government that has delayed RON95 subsidy reform four times, can’t decide when to float petrol prices, and just announced RM500 Hari Raya handouts days before a potential fiscal squeeze. ‘Prepared’ means nothing without detail. What is the fiscal buffer? At what oil price does the subsidy bill become unsustainable? What’s the contingency if RON95 has to be reformed during Ramadan? Zafrul knows these answers — his refusal to share them tells you the answers aren’t reassuring.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has claimed to be ‘prepared’ for external shocks. Najib said the same before 1MDB’s full impact hit. Muhyiddin said it during COVID before EPF withdrawals became the government’s emergency fund. The word ‘prepared’ in Malaysian politics means ‘we’ll figure it out when it happens.’

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The Deputy Economy Minister told a summit this same day that growth would be 4-4.5% — while the summit’s own chairman warned geopolitical tensions are ‘repricing risk across the entire region.’ Your advisor says you’re prepared; your minister says everything is fine; the market says otherwise. Pick a narrative and back it with data, not vibes.

Sources: Malay Mail | The Star

item.title: Anwar Admits He Can Only Hold RON95 Subsidy for 'One or Two Months' — While Fuel Prices Spike Across the Board
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-06

📌 What Happened

On March 6, 2026, Prime Minister and Finance Minister Anwar Ibrahim told reporters that Malaysia can maintain the subsidised RON95 petrol price at RM1.99 per litre under the Budi95 scheme — but only for ‘one or two months.’ His exact words: ‘We will try to control the effects of the Iranian conflict, including for RON95, which is RM1.99 per litre. We will monitor this situation because so far, we can still hold off for one or two months.’ This came the same week that RON97 petrol rose 10 sen to RM3.25 per litre, unsubsidised RON95 climbed 8 sen to RM2.67, and diesel in Peninsular Malaysia jumped 8 sen to RM3.12 — all driven by the escalating Middle East conflict and 200 vessels stuck in the Strait of Hormuz.

🔥 Why It Matters

This is the first time Anwar has publicly put a time limit on the government’s ability to hold fuel subsidies. The hedging language — ‘we will try,’ ‘hold off,’ ‘monitor’ — is a significant shift from previous assurances. If the Strait of Hormuz disruption continues, and 20% of the world’s oil supply remains at risk, the RM1.99 price becomes fiscally unsustainable. But Anwar cannot politically afford a fuel price hike — especially not with Hari Raya weeks away, snap election rumours swirling for July, and RM15 billion in STR cash handouts already deployed. He’s caught between fiscal reality and electoral survival, and the ‘one or two months’ language tells you which one he’s prioritising.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar spent three years telling Malaysians that Budi95’s targeted subsidy system was a masterstroke of fiscal reform — smarter, fairer, and more sustainable than blanket subsidies. Now, the moment global oil prices actually test the system, he’s already hedging and saying he can only hold it for a couple of months. What happened to the RM800 million in savings the government claimed from Budi95? What happened to Petronas’s RM32 billion dividend to the government? The truth is that the targeted subsidy was always designed for calm seas. The moment a real shock hits — which anyone tracking Middle East geopolitics could have predicted — the whole system starts to wobble. And instead of a plan, we get ‘we will try.’

📜 Previous Cases

This is a recurring pattern with Anwar on fuel policy: promise stability, deliver uncertainty. In early March, he told Dewan Negara the government would ’try to keep’ RON95 at RM1.99 — vague language that gave no timeline. Fuel prices have been creeping up steadily since January 2026, with diesel alone rising from RM2.84 in mid-January to RM3.12 by early March. Each week brings a new price adjustment upward, while the government insists the situation is ‘under control.’ It mirrors the BN-era playbook: reassure the public while the numbers quietly deteriorate, then act surprised when the dam breaks.

🎯 PH Callout

You told Malaysians that Budi95 was reform. You told them targeted subsidies would protect the rakyat while saving the government money. Now you’re admitting, publicly, that the system can only survive ‘one or two months’ of real global pressure. If this is fiscal reform, it’s built on cardboard. Where is the contingency plan? Where is the honest conversation with the public about what happens if oil hits USD100 a barrel and stays there? Stop managing headlines and start managing the economy. The rakyat deserves more than ‘we will try’ from the man who is simultaneously Prime Minister and Finance Minister.

Sources: The Star | paultan.org | FMT | The Edge | Bernama

item.title: Anwar Boasts RM426.7B 'Record' Approved Investments — But 'Approved' Doesn't Mean 'Arrived'
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-06

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On March 6, 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim announced that Malaysia recorded its highest-ever approved investments of RM426.7 billion in 2025 — an 11% increase from RM384.4 billion in 2024. According to MIDA, 8,390 projects were approved, with the potential to create 244,902 jobs. Anwar framed this as a vindication of his reform agenda and evidence of global confidence in Malaysia’s economic direction.

The announcement was made at the launch of the Capital Market Masterplan 2026-2030, with Anwar also serving as Finance Minister — a dual role he has held since taking power in 2022.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Because approved investments are not the same as realized investments, and the Malaysian public has been fed this distinction-free headline for decades.

MIDA tracks approvals — essentially, letters of intent and investment certificates. These are commitments on paper. The actual money flowing into the economy — foreign direct investment (FDI) realized — has historically been 50-60% of approved figures in Malaysia. That means up to half of these headline numbers may never translate into actual factories, jobs, or economic activity.

Anwar is using approved investment figures the same way every Malaysian PM before him has: as a political headline, not an economic reality check. The 244,902 jobs are potential jobs — not confirmed, not created, not guaranteed.

This matters because Malaysians are living through cost-of-living pressures, stagnant real wages, and subsidy rationalization. A headline number that may be half-real does not pay anyone’s bills.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Let’s be precise about what happened here: the Prime Minister stood at a podium and celebrated a number that he knows does not reflect actual investment.

Every MIDA annual report distinguishes between approved and realized investments. Every serious economist in Malaysia knows the realization gap. Anwar — a man who has been in and around Malaysian economic policymaking for three decades — knows this too.

And yet: no realization rate was disclosed. No breakdown of how much of 2023 or 2024’s approved investments actually materialized. No accountability metric. Just the biggest number, the best headline, and a claim of “global confidence.”

This is the economic equivalent of counting RSVPs as attendance. You don’t get to claim 244,902 jobs when those jobs exist only in approval letters. You don’t get to claim RM426.7 billion when a significant chunk of it may never arrive.

The UNCTAD Global Investment Trends Monitor (January 2026) itself noted that much of global FDI growth was concentrated in financial centres rather than productive assets. MIDA’s own press release acknowledged this — then quietly moved on to the headline number.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

This is not new. Every Malaysian government has played the approved-vs-realized game:

  • Under Najib, approved investments regularly topped RM200B+ annually, but realized FDI lagged significantly behind.
  • Under Mahathir 2.0, the same pattern continued — big approvals, quiet underperformance.
  • Anwar’s own government has not published a comprehensive, transparent realization audit since taking office. The pattern is always the same: announce the biggest possible number at a press conference, let the media run the headline, and never follow up with what actually happened. No PM has ever been held accountable for the gap between approved and realized investments.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You promised transparency and accountability as your brand. You said you were different from BN. You said you wouldn’t spin numbers.

So where is the realization report? Where is the breakdown of how much of 2023’s and 2024’s approved investments actually showed up as real money, real factories, and real jobs?

If you want credit for RM426.7 billion, show the receipts — not the invitations. Until then, this is the same economic theatre Malaysians have been watching since the 1990s, just with a reformist coat of paint.

Sources: Malay Mail | CNA | Bernama | The Star | MIDA Press Release

item.title: MACC Investigates Rafizi Over RM1.1 Billion ARM Holdings Deal — His Aide Flees to UK
item.description:
Said by: Rafizi Ramli, Former Economy Minister; Azam Baki, MACC Chief

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-04

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

In February-March 2026, MACC launched a formal investigation into a RM1.1 billion government deal with UK chip giant ARM Holdings, signed in March 2025 during Rafizi Ramli’s tenure as Economy Minister. The deal involved Malaysia Investment Development Authority (MIDA) obtaining intellectual property licences and compute subsystems valued at US$250 million, payable over 10 years.

MACC chief Azam Baki confirmed that 12 witnesses from various government agencies had given statements. More dramatically, MACC issued a public notice seeking Rafizi’s former aide, Chai Jin Shern (James Chai), who had left Malaysia and was working in the UK. MACC ordered Chai to return and cooperate with the investigation.

Rafizi called the investigation “politically motivated” and said it was “odd” that MACC hadn’t summoned him directly despite the probe running for over a month. He said both the Silicon Vision Programme and the National Energy Transition Roadmap went through multiple layers of government approval, including Cabinet endorsement.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

A RM1.1 billion deal is serious money. If there were elements of abuse of power, fraud, or governance failures in how this deal was structured, Malaysians deserve answers. The fact that Rafizi’s former aide has left the country and is unresponsive to MACC’s attempts to contact him raises obvious questions about what he might know.

But here’s the deeper problem: the timing stinks. This investigation comes after Rafizi lost his PKR deputy presidency race to Nurul Izzah and resigned from Cabinet. It comes while Azam Baki himself is under investigation for his own shareholding scandal. The appearance of MACC being weaponised against a political figure who just lost an internal power struggle is impossible to ignore.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

The bullshit here is layered. If the deal was genuinely problematic, why didn’t MACC investigate it when it was signed in March 2025? Why wait until Rafizi is out of Cabinet and politically weakened? The timing suggests this is either retaliatory or opportunistic — neither of which inspires confidence in MACC’s independence.

But Rafizi doesn’t get a pass either. His defence — “it went through Cabinet” — is exactly what every minister says when caught out. Cabinet approval doesn’t immunise a deal from scrutiny. And the fact that his aide fled the country is not a great look for someone who built his career on transparency and accountability.

The whole circus is a case study in Malaysian institutional failure: a compromised anti-corruption agency investigating a politically wounded reformist, while the PM calls Bloomberg a Zionist plot for exposing the MACC chief. Nobody comes out clean.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Malaysia has a long history of politically timed MACC investigations. Under Najib, MACC was accused of going after opposition figures while ignoring 1MDB. Under PH 1.0, MACC went after BN figures. Now, under PH 2.0, MACC appears to be going after a fallen PH insider. The institution remains a political tool regardless of who holds power.

Rafizi himself was once MACC’s loudest champion when it investigated BN. Now he’s calling the same agency politically motivated. The irony writes itself.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You promised an independent MACC. You kept Azam Baki — a man with two shareholding scandals — as chief. Now that same compromised MACC chief is investigating your own former minister in what looks like a politically timed probe. If you wanted Malaysians to trust MACC investigations, you should have cleaned house first. You can’t credibly investigate corruption when the head of your anti-corruption agency is himself under investigation. Fix the institution, then go after the wrongdoers. In that order.

Sources: Straits Times | CNA | FMT | The Edge | NST

item.title: Nearly RM1 Billion Paid in Court Damages for Government Negligence — Anwar's Fix? Letters of Reprimand
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister and Finance Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-04

📌 What Happened

Prime Minister and Finance Minister Anwar Ibrahim disclosed in a written parliamentary reply — responding to PH’s own William Leong (Selayang) — that the Malaysian government paid out RM933,360,633 in court-awarded damages between 2020 and 2025. The payouts were the result of negligence, misconduct, and other wrongdoing by government officials and agencies. Anwar outlined measures including instructing ministries to conduct investigations into the cases and “recommend disciplinary action” against those found negligent — and, spectacularly, issuing letters of reprimand.

🔥 Why It Matters

Nearly RM1 billion of public money was handed over because government officials were negligent, irresponsible, or outright corrupt in carrying out their duties — and the government’s proposed accountability mechanism is a sternly worded letter. This figure spans the administrations of Muhyiddin, Ismail Sabri, and now Anwar himself. The fact that this disclosure came buried in a written parliamentary reply rather than proactively announced tells you everything about how seriously this government takes fiscal accountability. PH promised to end the culture of impunity. Instead, it’s inherited it and furnished it with a fresh coat of bureaucratic language.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Letters of reprimand. For nearly a billion ringgit in losses caused by government negligence. Let that sink in. In the private sector, a single case of professional negligence resulting in significant financial loss would trigger terminations, lawsuits, and regulatory action. In Anwar’s Malaysia, it triggers a stern memo. This isn’t accountability — it’s theatre. If PH were serious about reform, it would name the ministries involved, disclose the individual cases, surcharge the officers responsible, and refer criminal negligence to the relevant authorities. Instead, the Prime Minister filed a quiet written reply and moved on. The rakyat pays the bill; nobody pays the price.

📜 Previous Cases

Malaysia has a long, inglorious history of government negligence costing taxpayers billions — from the PKFZ scandal (RM12.5 billion in cost overruns and soft loans) to the endless saga of abandoned government projects and botched procurement exercises under successive administrations. PH itself campaigned on ending this cycle in both 2018 and 2022. During PH 1.0 under Mahathir, the government conducted an audit of 1MDB-era spending but quietly shelved follow-up enforcement. Now under PH 2.0, Anwar’s government discloses the losses but proposes remedies so weak they amount to an invitation to keep doing it.

🎯 PH Callout

You promised the rakyat you would be different. You said there would be consequences. You said clean governance wasn’t a slogan — it was a commitment. So where are the consequences for nearly RM1 billion in court-ordered damages caused by your own government machinery? Letters of reprimand are what you give someone who parks in the wrong spot, not officials whose negligence cost the nation hundreds of millions. Name the ministries. Publish the cases. Surcharge the officers. Show the country that “Madani” means something beyond a logo on a government letterhead.

Sources: FMT — Govt paid nearly RM1bil in court-awarded damages between 2020 and 2025

item.title: 5G Dual Network Fiasco — DNB vs TM Contract War Over Termination
item.description:
Said by: Digital Nasional Bhd / Telekom Malaysia

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-03

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 3 March 2026, Telekom Malaysia (TM) attempted to terminate its 5G wholesale agreement with Digital Nasional Berhad (DNB), the government entity managing Malaysia’s 5G rollout. DNB rejected the termination, saying the contract runs until 2032 and remains binding. Malaysia’s dual 5G network strategy — introduced after Anwar’s government reversed the original single-network plan — has been plagued by policy flip-flops, RM2 billion in DNB debt, and the controversial awarding of the second 5G network to U Mobile.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Malaysia’s 5G policy has become a case study in how political interference destroys industrial strategy. The original single-network plan under DNB was already controversial. Anwar’s government then introduced a “dual network” approach — splitting the rollout between DNB and a consortium — which created contractual chaos, duplication of infrastructure, and uncertainty for telcos. Now TM and DNB are fighting in public over who owes what to whom. While politicians argue about contracts, Malaysians still have patchy 5G coverage and telcos still don’t have clarity on their obligations.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The dual network decision was widely seen as a political move to accommodate UMNO-linked business interests rather than a technical improvement. U Mobile’s selection for the second network was questioned by industry analysts. DNB’s RM2 billion debt was accumulated under a plan the government itself designed. Now the government is watching its own creation implode in a contractual dispute — and Malaysians are paying for it through delayed rollout and higher costs. This is what happens when telecoms policy is driven by patronage rather than engineering.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s telecoms sector has a long history of politically motivated decisions. The award of 3G and 4G licences was repeatedly criticised for favouring politically connected firms. DNB itself was created under Muhyiddin’s government with minimal transparency. Anwar’s dual network decision was supposed to fix this — instead, it doubled the problems. The pattern: create a government entity, load it with debt, let it fight with the private sector, blame the previous administration.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You inherited a flawed 5G plan and made it worse. The dual network was your decision. The contractual chaos is your responsibility. While DNB and TM fight over termination clauses, Malaysians are stuck with incomplete 5G infrastructure and a RM2 billion bill. Fix the policy, resolve the contracts, and stop letting political interests dictate telecoms strategy. Or admit that 5G in Malaysia is just another patronage project with better branding.

Sources: NST | CNA

item.title: The Scoreboard: Things That Were BETTER Under BN Than PH — An Uncomfortable Truth
item.description:
Said by: Comparative Economic Data / Various Sources

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-03-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: By multiple measurable metrics, ordinary Malaysians were objectively better off under certain BN policies than under PH: (1) GST was more efficient and less regressive than SST; (2) Diesel was RM2.15 vs RM3.35; (3) Festive toll-free periods existed; (4) PISA scores were 31 points higher in math; (5) Food prices were lower; (6) RON95 subsidy was universal without the anxiety of ’targeted’ reform; (7) Electricity for domestic use was GST-exempt.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The fundamental promise of changing government is that things will get BETTER, not worse. When multiple cost-of-living and quality-of-life indicators are worse under PH than under the government they replaced, the entire rationale for PH’s existence comes into question. Voters didn’t march for Reformasi so they could pay more for diesel.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH will argue context — global inflation, inherited debt, COVID aftermath. These are real factors. But they don’t explain why Malaysia’s food prices are the HIGHEST in Southeast Asia (surpassing richer Singapore), why PISA decline was DOUBLE the OECD average, or why diesel had to rise 56% in one shot while JAKIM kept RM2.6 billion. Context explains some decline; it doesn’t excuse all of it. Choices were made, and those choices made life worse.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: BN was voted out for corruption, abuse of power, and 1MDB — all legitimate reasons. But the implicit promise was that PH would maintain or improve the baseline while cleaning up governance. Instead, governance remains corrupt (Shamsul Iskandar, MACC scandal, GLC patronage) AND daily life costs more. You got the worst of both worlds: BN-level corruption with higher costs.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Here’s your report card. Diesel: WORSE. Food prices: WORSE. Education outcomes: WORSE. Press freedom: SAME or WORSE. Corruption: SAME. Cost of living: WORSE. Toll abolition: BROKEN. EPF protection: WEAKER. Healthcare: WORSE. The one metric where you’re better? PR and speechwriting. Congratulations — your press conferences are excellent. Too bad Malaysians can’t eat press conferences.

Sources: Paul Tan — RON95 Subsidy Analysis | ISIS Malaysia — PISA Score Decline

item.title: Zafrul vs Rafizi — Former MITI Minister Tells Rafizi to 'Stop Spinning' Over Double Tariff Claims
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MIDA Chairman / former MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-02-23

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 23 February 2026, Tengku Zafrul — now MIDA chairman — publicly rebuked former Economy Minister Rafizi Ramli for claiming Malaysia faced nearly double the tariff rate under the US trade deal. Zafrul accused Rafizi of ‘distorting the facts’ and told him to ‘stop spinning — it’s exhausting to keep responding.’ Rafizi had argued the ART locked Malaysia into unfavourable terms. Zafrul countered that the 19% tariff no longer applied after the US Supreme Court struck down Trump’s tariff framework.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Two former PH ministers are publicly fighting over a trade deal the government signed. Zafrul negotiated it; Rafizi — who was ousted from Cabinet — is now attacking it. This isn’t policy debate; it’s a proxy war between PH factions using trade policy as ammunition. When your own former ministers can’t agree on whether a deal you signed was good or bad, the rakyat has every right to ask: who was actually in charge of trade policy?

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Both men are right and wrong simultaneously. Zafrul is right that Rafizi’s specific tariff claim was technically inaccurate. Rafizi is right that the broader deal surrendered too much. But neither is addressing the real problem: the deal was rushed, poorly communicated, and published on the White House website before Malaysian MPs saw it. The public spat between two PH elites is entertainment, not accountability.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The Zafrul-Rafizi rivalry predates this deal. Rafizi publicly warned that Farhash allegations undermined the PM’s credibility; Zafrul stayed silent. Now Rafizi attacks Zafrul’s signature achievement. PH’s internal dysfunction is becoming its defining feature — they fight each other more effectively than they fight corruption.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: When your former ministers are publicly calling each other liars over a trade deal you signed four months ago, the problem isn’t the tariff rate — it’s the coalition. Fix your internal communications before you negotiate with superpowers. And stop making the rakyat watch your dirty laundry air on Facebook.

Sources: FMT | The Vibes | Bernama

item.title: MAHB Delisted After GDA Takeover — Five Directors Resign in Protest
item.description:
Said by: Bursa Malaysia / GDA Consortium

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-02-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 18 February 2026, Malaysia Airports Holdings Berhad (MAHB) — the public airport operator managing 39 airports nationwide — was officially delisted from Bursa Malaysia after the GDA consortium completed its takeover at RM11 per share. The consortium was led by Khazanah Nasional and EPF, with BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP) as a key partner. All five independent directors resigned in protest, having deemed the offer “unfair and unreasonable” to minority shareholders. Hong Leong Investment Bank’s independent assessment concluded the offer was “not fair but reasonable” — a diplomatic way of saying shareholders were getting shortchanged but had no better option. Civil society groups protested BlackRock’s GIP involvement amid Palestine boycott concerns.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A strategic national asset — the operator of every commercial airport in Malaysia — is now privately controlled with drastically reduced public accountability. When MAHB was listed, its financials, board decisions, and performance were subject to Bursa disclosure rules and public scrutiny. That transparency is gone. The mass resignation of all five independent directors is extraordinary and nearly unprecedented in Malaysian corporate history. These are professionals who staked their reputations on saying the deal was bad for minority shareholders. Yet the government-linked consortium pushed it through anyway. When the people whose job is to protect shareholders resign in protest, the message is unambiguous.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government used public money (Khazanah and EPF are government-controlled) to take private a public asset — reducing accountability while using rakyat’s retirement savings to fund the deal. EPF manages the savings of millions of Malaysian workers. Those workers are now involuntary investors in a delisted company with no market price transparency and no public reporting requirements. The “not fair but reasonable” assessment is corporate speak for “you’re getting a bad deal but you can’t stop it.” And the Palestine boycott angle — BlackRock’s GIP involvement drew protests from the same communities Anwar courts on Gaza solidarity. The government that lectures the world about Palestine partnered with BlackRock to privatise Malaysian airports. The hypocrisy is breathtaking.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a long history of controversial privatisations and delistings of strategic assets. Tenaga Nasional, Telekom Malaysia, and various highway concessionaires have all been subject to ownership restructuring that reduced public oversight. The MAHB delisting follows the same playbook: government-linked entities acquire public companies at prices independent assessors deem unfair, minority shareholders are squeezed out, and accountability disappears behind private boardroom doors. Under BN, this was called cronyism. Under PH, it is called “strategic restructuring.”

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Five independent directors resigned saying the deal was unfair. Hong Leong said it was “not fair.” You used EPF — workers’ retirement money — to fund a deal that independent voices said was bad for shareholders. MAHB manages every airport in Malaysia; Malaysians deserve transparency over how it is run. Commit to publishing MAHB’s financials annually even after delisting. Commit to independent board oversight. Or admit that this was a deal designed to benefit the consortium at the expense of the public — exactly the kind of crony capitalism you promised to end.

Sources: The Edge | Malay Mail

item.title: Malaysia Study: Cost-Benefit Analysis of US Trade Deal Shows 'Concerns' But Government Says Don't Quit
item.description:
Said by: Malaysian Government

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-01-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In January 2026, Bloomberg reported that the Malaysian government announced a formal cost-benefit analysis of the controversial US-Malaysia Agreed Reciprocal Trade (ART) agreement — following months of mounting criticism from former AG Tommy Thomas (who called it “the worst agreement since independence”), former PM Mahathir, think tanks, and opposition politicians. But in the same breath, the government warned lawmakers not to quit the pact. East Asia Forum published a detailed analysis titled “The Art of the Deal Lost in US-Malaysia Pact,” arguing Malaysia surrendered critical mineral export controls, digital services taxation rights, and independent trade policy in exchange for a tariff reduction from 25% to 19% — still punitive by any standard. The deal’s details were first published on the White House website before being shared with Malaysian Parliament.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Announcing a cost-benefit analysis while pre-empting its conclusion is not governance — it is political theatre. “Study but don’t quit” tells you everything about this administration’s approach: acknowledge problems, change nothing. If the analysis shows the deal hurts Malaysia, will the government act? The answer is already in the framing. The study exists to absorb pressure, not to inform decisions. The fact that Parliament learned about the deal from the White House website is a governance failure of the first order — trade agreements that constrain national sovereignty should require parliamentary approval before signing, not after.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: “Study but don’t quit” is the Madani playbook in miniature: acknowledge the problem, promise a process, change nothing. It is the same approach applied to the AG-PP separation, UUCA repeal, toll abolition, and every other stalled reform. The cost-benefit analysis will either be buried, selectively quoted, or used to justify minor adjustments while the core agreement remains untouched. Anwar bypassed the legislature on the most consequential economic agreement in decades. When your own former AG calls your trade deal the worst since independence and your response is “we’ll study it but we’re keeping it,” the study is performative.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a history of trade agreements where the government commits first and reviews later. The TPPA under Najib faced similar criticism — Malaysia signed on to provisions critics said undermined sovereignty, pharmaceutical pricing, and labour rights. PH 1.0 withdrew from the TPPA in 2018, calling it unfair. PH 2.0 signed the ART — which critics say contains even more restrictive provisions — and now wants to “study” whether it was a good idea. The pattern of committing to deals under pressure, then conducting post-hoc reviews that change nothing, is a bipartisan Malaysian tradition.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: If you’re serious about the cost-benefit analysis, publish the full terms of the ART — not the White House summary, but the actual agreement. Let independent economists, not government-appointed task forces, evaluate the deal. If the analysis shows Malaysia got a bad deal, have the courage to renegotiate. “Study but don’t quit” is not a policy — it is a confession that you already know the answer and don’t want to act on it. Parliament deserves a vote on trade agreements that constrain national sovereignty. Give them one.

Sources: Bloomberg | East Asia Forum

item.title: Wages Haven't Kept Up with Costs — Real Purchasing Power Has DECLINED Under PH
item.description:
Said by: DOSM / BNM / Consumer Groups

Category: Economy

Date: 2026-01-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: While nominal wages have increased slightly under PH — minimum wage raised to RM1,500, modest salary increments across sectors — the cost of living has risen faster. Diesel is up 56%. SST was expanded to cover more essentials. Food prices are the highest in Southeast Asia at $1,940 per person per year according to USDA data. Electricity costs are up. Property prices are up. The result: real purchasing power — what your ringgit actually buys — has declined for most Malaysians. A 2025 consumer survey found 77% of Malaysians say costs have risen significantly, while official inflation claims just 1.3%.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Purchasing power is the truest measure of economic wellbeing. GDP growth, investment figures, and export numbers are abstractions. What matters to a family is whether they can afford the same basket of groceries this month as last month. Under PH, the answer is increasingly no. When 71.6% of household income goes to daily necessities, disposable income for savings, education, and emergencies shrinks to almost nothing. Families are one medical emergency away from crisis. The government celebrates macro numbers while the rakyat counts sen at the checkout counter.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government points to headline inflation of around 1.3% and claims prices are “stable.” But official CPI calculations underweight items that have risen most sharply — food, fuel, services — and don’t capture the “felt” inflation that families experience. When 77% of Malaysians say costs have risen significantly but official inflation says 1.3%, somebody is lying — and it’s not the 77%. Food away from home inflation runs at 4.3-4.7%, far exceeding the headline rate. The government is using statistical methodology to gaslight the population about their own lived experience. Meanwhile, PH abolished GST (which zero-rated essentials) and replaced it with SST, which taxes MORE daily items. Under BN, diesel was RM2.15. Under PH, it’s RM3.35.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN’s GST system, essentials were zero-rated — consumers paid no tax on basic food, domestic electricity, and public transport. PH abolished GST as a populist move and replaced it with SST, which is less efficient and taxes more daily items. The uncomfortable truth: by many daily-life cost measures, BN’s baseline was better than PH’s performance. Every Malaysian government has faced cost-of-living complaints, but PH uniquely promised to make life cheaper — then made it more expensive through diesel float, SST expansion, and subsidy rationalisation without adequate targeted relief.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised to make life better for ordinary Malaysians. Life is MORE expensive. Diesel costs MORE. Food costs MORE. Services cost MORE through expanded SST. Electricity costs MORE. And wages haven’t kept up. Every Malaysian family knows this — they see it every time they buy groceries, fill their tank, or pay their bills. Your 1.3% inflation statistic is an insult to the 77% of Malaysians who know their money buys less than it did three years ago. Stop hiding behind macro numbers and address the lived reality of the rakyat.

Sources: Paul Tan | Reuters

item.title: Public Transport 'Functional But Fragile' — One Disruption and Everyone Drives
item.description:
Said by: Khazanah Research Institute (KRI)

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-12-31

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: A Khazanah Research Institute (KRI) study published in late December 2025 found that Greater KL’s public transport system is “functional but fragile” — minor disruptions cause commuters to abandon it entirely for private vehicles. KRI concluded that rail was “never meant to fix Malaysia’s traffic problem.” The study found that fuel remains artificially cheap, parking is abundant and affordable, walking environments are fragmented and hostile, and last-mile connectivity is inadequate. FMT’s analysis argued it is “an unfinished system, not infrastructure, that fails Malaysian commuters.” Yet the government continues announcing mega rail projects — MRT3, LRT3, ECRL — as if building more tracks alone will solve mobility.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Successive governments, including PH, keep announcing multi-billion ringgit rail projects as if infrastructure alone solves mobility. KRI’s own study — funded by the government’s own sovereign wealth fund — says it doesn’t. Without simultaneously reforming urban planning, parking policy, fuel pricing, and last-mile connectivity, every new rail line is just an expensive band-aid on a systemic failure. Billions are spent on tracks while the system remains one disruption away from collapse. Commuters who try public transport once and get stranded never come back.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government subsidises fuel (keeping driving artificially cheap), allows abundant free parking in urban areas (removing the cost incentive to use public transport), neglects pedestrian infrastructure (making it dangerous to walk to stations), and fails to invest in last-mile solutions (leaving commuters stranded between stations and destinations). Then it announces another RM50 billion rail project and claims it is “solving traffic.” This is not a transport policy — it is an infrastructure spending programme that benefits construction companies and politicians who cut ribbons, not commuters who need to get to work. LRT3 has been delayed repeatedly. MRT3 costs keep escalating. The ECRL’s ridership projections are fantasy. KRI told the government the approach doesn’t work, and the government responded by announcing more of the same.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has been building rail infrastructure for decades — KTM Komuter, LRT, Monorail, MRT1, MRT2 — yet car ownership rates keep climbing and traffic congestion keeps worsening. Each new project is announced with the same promise: this one will finally solve traffic. None has. The reason is structural: Malaysia’s urban planning is car-centric by design. Without changing the design — restricting parking, raising fuel costs, improving walkability, building bus networks — adding rail lines is like building a bigger bucket while the tap keeps running.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your own government-funded think tank says rail alone doesn’t solve traffic. Yet your transport policy is: build more rail. Reform parking policy. Reform fuel pricing. Fund bus networks. Fix pedestrian infrastructure. These are the boring, politically difficult changes that would actually make public transport work. But they don’t generate ribbon-cutting photo ops, so they don’t happen. Stop building expensive monuments to political vanity and start building a transport SYSTEM.

Sources: Malay Mail | FMT

item.title: Toll Abolition Promise Broken — Govt Pays RM591M to FREEZE Hikes Instead
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-12-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: PH promised to abolish highway tolls in both GE14 and GE15. Instead, in December 2025, the government announced it would spend RM591 million just to prevent toll increases on 10 highways in 2026 — paying concessionaires with public money to not raise prices. The Works Minister admitted that fully abolishing tolls would cost “billions.” Gerakan mocked the broken promise. FMT asked whether Anwar would “repeat PH’s pledge to abolish tolls” ahead of GE16. Malaysian drivers continue paying tolls AND their taxes now subsidise the freeze. Festive toll-free periods were also cancelled.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Toll abolition was one of PH’s most concrete, measurable manifesto promises — specific enough that voters could easily verify delivery. The reality: not only were tolls not abolished, the government is paying highway concessionaires hundreds of millions annually just to freeze rate hikes. The RM591M spent on freezing could have funded actual infrastructure improvements. Instead, it flows to concessionaire shareholders — politically connected companies that profit from a captive public. This is the worst possible outcome: broken promise, continued tolls, AND public money transferred to private companies.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The Works Minister’s admission that abolition would cost “billions” is an admission that PH made the promise knowing it was undeliverable. You can’t campaign on toll abolition twice, win government twice, and then say it costs too much. Either you knew the cost before promising (in which case you lied to voters) or you didn’t bother calculating it (in which case you’re incompetent). The toll system represents a regressive tax on mobility — lower-income workers who commute long distances to factory jobs pay a disproportionate share of their income on tolls. PH’s response to this inequality: pay concessionaires with tax money to maintain the status quo.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The toll abolition promise has been made by every opposition coalition since the early 2000s. PH 1.0 (2018-2020) didn’t abolish tolls either — despite 22 months in government. The concession agreements, many awarded under previous administrations through questionable processes, are structured to guarantee private profits at public expense. No government has had the political will to renegotiate or buy back concessions. The concessionaires know this, which is why they keep raising toll rates and governments keep paying them not to.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised to abolish tolls. Twice. In two general elections. You’ve now been in power for over three years. Tolls are still there. You’re spending RM591M of public money to FREEZE them — not abolish them, freeze them. That RM591M goes to concessionaire shareholders, not to the rakyat. Stop making promises you know you can’t keep. If toll abolition is unaffordable, say so honestly — and explain your alternative plan. Or better yet, renegotiate the concession agreements that lock Malaysia into paying private companies forever. You won’t, because the concessionaires are politically connected. So the rakyat pays twice: once at the toll booth, once through taxes.

Sources: The Edge | FMT

item.title: LRT3 Delayed AGAIN — Software Glitches, Failed Test Runs, RM475M Penalties Looming
item.description:
Said by: Anthony Loke, Transport Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-12-15

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: The LRT3 line from Bandar Utama to Johan Setia — a multi-billion ringgit project intended to transform connectivity in the Klang Valley — has been postponed repeatedly due to signalling software failures and the inability to complete fault-free test runs. Transport Minister Anthony Loke warned contractors may face penalties exceeding RM475 million for the delays. The opening date remains unconfirmed. The Edge and Lowyat both reported the latest round of testing setbacks as the system failed to achieve the required reliability benchmarks.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: LRT3 was supposed to be operational years ago. Repeated delays mean commuters continue relying on cars, worsening congestion and pollution in the Klang Valley. Public money is tied up in a system nobody can use — billions invested with zero return to commuters. The signalling software failures suggest deeper issues with project management, contractor capability, and government oversight. Meanwhile, commuters who were told LRT3 would change their lives continue sitting in traffic.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Loke’s penalty threats ring hollow. The government has a track record of renegotiating rather than enforcing consequences on mega-project contractors. Penalties get waived, deadlines get extended, cost overruns get absorbed by the public, and nobody is held accountable. Infrastructure delays under PH are no different from infrastructure delays under BN — the “reform” government delivers the same results with the same contractors and the same excuses. If the RM475M in penalties are actually enforced, it would be the first time in Malaysian infrastructure history. Don’t hold your breath.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s mega-infrastructure project history is a catalogue of delays, cost overruns, and renegotiated contracts. MRT2 was delayed. The ECRL was renegotiated (costs ballooned from RM55B to reportedly higher). The Penang Transport Master Plan is stalled. LRT3 now joins the list. The pattern across administrations: announce ambitious projects, award contracts to politically connected firms, miss deadlines, threaten penalties, renegotiate quietly, and repeat. The contractors get paid regardless. The public gets delays regardless. Only the minister cutting the ribbon changes.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: If RM475M in penalties are owed, collect them. If the contractor can’t deliver, replace them. If the signalling software doesn’t work after years of testing, find out why and publish the findings. Stop threatening consequences you never enforce. Infrastructure accountability starts with one simple principle: contracts that aren’t met should have real consequences. Under BN, they didn’t. Under PH, they still don’t. Prove us wrong. Enforce the RM475M. We dare you.

Sources: The Edge | Lowyat

item.title: 1MDB Debt Still RM50 Billion — Malaysia's Largest Fiscal Burden Under Anwar's Watch
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-12-03

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: As of December 2025, Malaysia’s 1MDB debt still stands at approximately RM50 billion — the largest single fiscal burden in the country’s history. RM42 billion has already been paid in principal and interest. RM29 billion has been recovered through enforcement actions. Approximately RM13 billion remains to be settled.
