Under Anwar’s watch, Najib Razak — the PM who presided over 1MDB — had his jail sentence halved by the Pardons Board and was subsequently granted house arrest. The rakyat continues to pay the debt while the man responsible serves a comfortable sentence.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Every ringgit spent servicing 1MDB debt is a ringgit not spent on schools, hospitals, infrastructure, or social protection. The RM50 billion total — principal plus interest — represents the largest single act of kleptocracy in Malaysian history. Anwar made 1MDB accountability his signature promise. Instead, Najib got a halved sentence, house arrest, and whispers of a political return. The debt remains. The accountability was diluted. The rakyat pays.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar built his political career on holding Najib accountable for 1MDB. “Kerajaan Madani” was supposed to mean no impunity for corruption. Instead, under Anwar’s government: the Pardons Board halved Najib’s sentence, the courts granted house arrest, and political allies openly discuss Najib’s “contribution” to UMNO. The man who stole billions lives comfortably while 33 million Malaysians service the debt he created. This is not accountability — it is accommodation.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: 1MDB has been investigated on four continents. The US DOJ recovered over $1 billion in assets. Singapore jailed bankers. Switzerland sanctioned institutions. Goldman Sachs paid $3.9 billion in settlements. Malaysia — the country that was robbed — has done the least of any jurisdiction involved. Under Najib, the investigation was suppressed. Under Muhyiddin, it continued slowly. Under Anwar, the investigation supposedly concluded but the principal figure serves a reduced, comfortable sentence.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You promised 1MDB accountability. You delivered a halved sentence and house arrest for the man who presided over the theft. RM50 billion in total cost. RM13 billion still owed. And Najib may return to politics. If this is what accountability looks like under Kerajaan Madani, the word has lost all meaning. Ensure every ringgit of 1MDB debt recovery is publicly accounted for. Block any attempt at a full pardon. Or admit that 1MDB accountability was a campaign slogan, not a governance commitment.

Sources: Reuters | Malaysiakini | DOJ

item.title: Debt-to-GDP Still at 64.6% — Anwar Says Wait Until 2028 for Relief
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-12-02

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s debt-to-GDP ratio stood at 64.6% in 2024 — above the government’s own 60% target ceiling set under the Public Finance and Fiscal Responsibility Act. In December 2025, Anwar told Bloomberg that debt pressure would “ease from end-2028” — six years into his tenure before fiscal health even begins to improve. New borrowings were reduced to RM75 billion from RM100 billion but remain massive. The 1MDB legacy alone still accounts for RM50 billion in liabilities. CNA reported on Malaysia’s “increased spending” alongside the “low debt ratio” aspiration that remains unfulfilled.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Anwar campaigned on fiscal responsibility and ending the debt culture of previous administrations. Instead, his government is borrowing at near-record levels while 1MDB alone still accounts for RM50 billion in liabilities. Debt-to-GDP above 60% means Malaysia’s fiscal buffer against economic shocks is dangerously thin. If a global recession hits, or commodity prices crash, or a pandemic returns, the government has limited room to respond. Telling Malaysians to wait until 2028 for fiscal relief is asking them to trust a PM who may not even be in power by then.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Fiscal discipline means making hard choices now — not pushing timelines to the next election cycle. The 2028 target conveniently falls after GE16, meaning Anwar wants voters to re-elect him on the promise that fiscal improvement is “just around the corner” — the same promise he made in 2022. Meanwhile, spending continues to rise: civil service wages (1.6 million employees), subsidies (even after “rationalisation”), development projects (many driven by political rather than economic logic), and debt servicing (16% of revenue). The structural deficit means every year the government spends more than it earns, and borrows the difference. PH has not fundamentally changed this equation — it has just changed the rhetoric around it.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has promised fiscal responsibility while borrowing heavily. Najib’s 1MDB created the RM50 billion legacy that still haunts the books. Muhyiddin’s pandemic spending was necessary but poorly tracked. Anwar inherited a mess — that’s true. But three years is enough time to demonstrate a credible path to fiscal consolidation. Instead, the target keeps moving: first it was 2025, then 2027, now 2028. The can gets kicked, the debt compounds, and the next government inherits an even bigger mess.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You’ve been in power for over three years. The debt-to-GDP ratio is STILL above your own 60% target. You’re telling Malaysians to wait until 2028 — conveniently after the next election — for relief. That’s not a fiscal plan, it’s an electoral strategy. Cut spending that doesn’t serve the public (238 political appointments, RM85.5M J-Kom propaganda, RM2.6B JAKIM). Enforce tax compliance on the wealthy and corporations. Stop borrowing to fund political patronage. Or admit that fiscal responsibility, like every other reform promise, is permanently “coming soon.”

Sources: Bloomberg | CNA

item.title: Highway Concessions: Rakyat Pays Tolls to Make Cronies Rich — Toll Abolition Promise Broken AGAIN
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim Administration / Transport Ministry

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: The PH government’s core manifesto promise to abolish or significantly reduce highway tolls has been systematically broken. Instead of toll abolition, the government spent RM591 million in 2025 to merely freeze toll hikes — paying concessionaires with public money to not raise prices they shouldn’t be charging in the first place. Festive toll-free periods were cancelled. The fundamental structure of highway concessions — where private companies collect guaranteed revenue from a captive public while the government underwrites their profits — remains completely unchanged. Abolishing tolls was a core PH campaign promise in both GE14 (2018) and GE15 (2022). Three years into government, the promise has been quietly shelved with no accountability.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The toll system represents a regressive tax on mobility: lower-income workers who commute long distances to factory jobs in industrial areas pay a disproportionate share of their income on tolls. Highway concessions, many awarded under previous administrations through questionable processes, generate enormous profits for politically connected companies. The government claims it cannot afford to buy back concessions, yet finds money for political appointments, propaganda, and JAKIM. The rakyat pays twice: once at the toll booth, once through taxes that subsidise the freeze.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: You promised to abolish tolls. Twice. In two general elections. Instead, you’re paying private companies RM591 million of public money to maintain the status quo. This is the worst possible outcome: broken promise, continued tolls, AND public money transferred to concessionaire shareholders. The concession agreements are structured to guarantee private profits at public expense — guaranteed traffic volumes, guaranteed returns, government compensation for shortfalls. The concessionaires literally cannot lose. And the “reform” government has done nothing to change this structure. Not renegotiation. Not buyback. Not restructuring. Nothing.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The toll abolition promise has been made by every opposition coalition since the early 2000s. PH 1.0 (2018-2020) didn’t abolish tolls despite 22 months in government. PH 2.0 has now been in power longer and still hasn’t delivered. The concessionaires — many linked to politically connected businesspeople — know the pattern: politicians promise abolition to win votes, then discover it’s “too expensive” once in office. The tolls continue. The profits flow. The cycle repeats. And by GE16, PH will probably promise toll abolition again.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: How many times can you promise to abolish tolls and not deliver before voters stop believing you? You’ve now spent more public money FREEZING toll hikes than some countries spend building entire road networks. Renegotiate the concession agreements. Publish the terms — let Malaysians see what their government signed them up for. If the agreements are so lopsided that buying them back costs “billions,” that itself is the scandal. Who negotiated these deals? Who benefited? And why has your government done nothing to fix them in three years?

Sources: Paul Tan

item.title: National Debt Servicing Eats 16% of Revenue — Every Sixth Ringgit Goes to Paying Interest
item.description:
Said by: Ministry of Finance / Bloomberg

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s debt servicing costs consume approximately 16% of federal government revenue — meaning roughly every sixth ringgit collected in taxes goes straight to paying interest on accumulated debt rather than funding schools, hospitals, or infrastructure. With the debt-to-GDP ratio at 64.6% — above the government’s own 60% target — and 1MDB legacy debt still totalling RM50 billion on the books, fiscal space remains severely constrained. Anwar told Bloomberg that debt pressure would only “ease from end-2028.” The Star reported that 1MDB debt remains “Malaysia’s largest fiscal burden.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Debt servicing at 16% of revenue is a silent tax on the future. Every ringgit spent on interest is a ringgit NOT spent on healthcare (where 33,000 doctors are on temporary contracts), education (where PISA scores are declining), infrastructure (where LRT3 is delayed and roads crumble), or social safety nets (where B40 families struggle with costs). The compound effect is devastating: high debt requires high servicing, which reduces fiscal space, which forces more borrowing, which increases future servicing costs. It’s a fiscal doom loop — and PH has done nothing to break it.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government continues to find new spending commitments — subsidies, civil service wages, development projects, 238 political appointments to GLCs — that make fiscal consolidation an ever-receding target. The structural deficit means future generations of Malaysians will inherit a mountain of debt for spending decisions made today, with compound interest ensuring the problem only grows. When Anwar says debt pressure will “ease from end-2028,” he’s asking Malaysians to accept six years of fiscal pain for a promise of eventual improvement. That’s not a plan — it’s a hope, and hope compounds about as well as debt does.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s debt burden is not new, but it has been consistently worsened by political spending. 1MDB added RM50 billion. Pandemic stimulus under Muhyiddin added tens of billions more. Subsidy commitments under every government add annual billions. The Public Finance and Fiscal Responsibility Act was supposed to impose discipline — but the 60% debt-to-GDP target has been breached without consequence. The act has no enforcement mechanism, making it aspirational rather than binding. Every government passes fiscal responsibility laws and then ignores them.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Every sixth ringgit of tax revenue goes to paying interest — not building hospitals, not hiring teachers, not fixing roads. 16% of revenue servicing debt is a governance failure that spans administrations, but you’ve been in power long enough to own it now. Cut wasteful spending: 238 political GLC appointments, RM85.5M propaganda budgets, and discretionary allocations that serve politics rather than people. Enforce corporate tax compliance. Publish a credible fiscal consolidation roadmap with annual targets. Or keep kicking the can to 2028 — and let the next generation pay the bill.

Sources: Bloomberg | The Star

item.title: Felda-Terengganu Land War — 15,000 Hectares Exploited for 40 Years Without Paying the State a Sen
item.description:
Said by: Terengganu State Government / Felda

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-30

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: A decades-long land dispute between the Federal Land Development Authority (Felda) and the Terengganu state government exploded in late November 2025 when the state issued trespass notices covering 10 plantations totalling approximately 15,000 hectares. Terengganu accused Felda of developing state land for over 40 years without paying premiums, taxes, or profit shares — essentially extracting billions in palm oil revenue from state land for free. FGV Holdings was also served notices. DPM Zahid eventually stepped in for negotiations.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: This is not a minor bureaucratic dispute — it is a case of a federal agency extracting billions from a state for four decades without compensation. The Terengganu state Exco accused Felda of using settlers as “human shields,” placing ordinary farmers in the middle of what is fundamentally a corporate land grab. States like Pahang and Sabah had received their payments — Terengganu was simply being ignored. The selective treatment raises the question: does the federal government treat PN-governed states differently from PH-governed ones?

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Terengganu demanded 30% of Felda’s gross profits in 2024 but Felda refused to even propose a counter-figure. The federal government — under PH oversight — allowed a GLC to freeload on state land for four decades while settlers bore the reputational cost. Only when Terengganu threatened eviction did DPM Zahid step in. That’s not governance — that’s damage control triggered by embarrassment. If this were a PH-governed state, the federal response would have been immediate. The delay tells you everything about how this government treats opposition states.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Federal-state land disputes are a recurring theme in Malaysian politics, and they almost always break along political lines. Kelantan’s oil royalty dispute with Petronas dragged on for decades under BN. Sabah and Sarawak have fought for resource rights since Malaysia’s formation. The pattern is consistent: federal agencies extract, states complain, and resolution only comes when political pressure becomes unbearable. Felda itself has a long history of mismanagement — RM4 billion in questionable investments under FGV, settler land schemes that enriched intermediaries rather than farmers.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You preach federalism and fair treatment of all states. But when it’s a PN-governed state being exploited by a federal agency, you looked the other way for years. Felda’s behaviour under your watch is indistinguishable from colonial extraction — take the resources, ignore the locals, and hide behind the settlers when caught. Pay Terengganu what it is owed. Audit every Felda operation on state land nationwide. Publish the revenue extracted versus premiums paid for every state. Or admit that your federalism only applies to states that voted for you.

Sources: Reuters | FMT | The Vibes

item.title: Penang PTMP Procurement: No Open Tenders, Just 'Request for Proposals' for Billions in Contracts
item.description:
Said by: Chow Kon Yeow / DAP Penang / Civil Society Critics

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-21

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Multiple reports, including from FMT and the academic community, documented that DAP’s Penang Transport Master Plan (PTMP) relied on ‘request-for-proposal’ (RFP) procurement mechanisms rather than open competitive tenders for billions of ringgit in infrastructure contracts. Civil society groups criticised this as opaque and developer-friendly. The PTMP includes the undersea tunnel (now under corruption trial), the Mutiara LRT, and various road projects — all procured without the standard open tender process that PH demands from the federal government.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Open competitive tendering is the gold standard for public procurement — it ensures the best price, prevents corruption, and allows public scrutiny. DAP demands open tenders from the federal government for every contract above RM50 million. But in Penang, where DAP has total control, billions in infrastructure contracts were awarded through RFP mechanisms that allow the state to pre-select bidders. The hypocrisy is structural, not incidental.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: DAP’s position on procurement is: open tenders for everyone else, RFPs for us. The party that lectured BN for decades about procurement transparency adopted the same opaque mechanisms when it controlled the purse strings. The undersea tunnel — the PTMP’s flagship project — is now a corruption case. MACC witnesses described it as a ’land scam.’ These are the outcomes of opaque procurement. DAP created the conditions for corruption by avoiding the safeguards it demands from others.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every major corruption scandal in Malaysian infrastructure — from 1MDB to PKFZ to the Pandora Papers — involved opaque procurement. DAP’s response to each was to demand transparency and open tenders. Yet in Penang, the same party used RFPs for its most expensive projects. The evidence from the Guan Eng trial suggests the procurement process was exploited for personal benefit.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You demand open tenders from Putrajaya. You use RFPs in Penang. Your former CM is on trial over a project procured through your preferred opaque method. The lesson is obvious: adopt open competitive tendering for all state projects above RM10 million. Publish all procurement documents. And stop using a procurement method that your own corruption case demonstrates can be exploited.

Sources: FMT

item.title: Data Centre Boom Drains Water and Power — Johor Told to Wait Until 2027 for Water Supply
item.description:
Said by: Johor state government and Anwar Ibrahim

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s data centre gold rush hit a wall in late 2025 when Johor — ground zero for the country’s silicon valley ambitions — told investors to postpone water-cooled projects for at least 18 months because the state couldn’t supply enough water. By February 2026, Anwar froze all non-AI data centre approvals entirely. Between 2023 and 2025, the government had approved billions in data centre projects from Microsoft, Google, AWS, Oracle, ByteDance, and GDS Holdings — without ensuring the infrastructure could support them. Malaysia’s national non-revenue water rate is 37.1%, meaning over a third of treated water is lost to leaking pipes before reaching consumers.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The government celebrated billions in data centre investment commitments as proof that the Madani economy was attracting global capital. Now it’s telling those same investors to wait because there’s no water and not enough electricity. You cannot approve massive industrial projects that each consume as much water as a small city without ensuring the infrastructure can support them. This is basic governance, and the government failed at it because investment announcements make better headlines than infrastructure audits. The freeze creates investor uncertainty at exactly the wrong moment — companies that committed billions based on government approvals are now being told to wait.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government simultaneously approved a Johor special economic zone designed to attract even more industrial investment, creating a direct conflict between economic ambition and infrastructure reality. Malaysia loses 37.1% of treated water to leaking pipes. States like Perlis and Kelantan waste 50-60%. Building water-intensive industries on top of a crumbling water system is negligence. The irony is suffocating: the government that can’t maintain water pipes for its own citizens approved water-guzzling data centres for foreign corporations, then had to tell both groups to wait. Governance by headline, failure by infrastructure.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a pattern of approving mega-projects before building the infrastructure to support them. The Iskandar Malaysia development zone promised a “world-class city” but still struggles with basic services. Forest City was approved as a RM400 billion development but sits largely empty. Each time, the government prioritises investment announcements over infrastructure planning, then scrambles when reality catches up. The data centre freeze is the latest iteration of the same pattern: approve first, plan never.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You approved billions in data centre investment without ensuring water and electricity supply. Now you’re telling investors to wait 18 months. Fix the pipes first — 37.1% non-revenue water is a national embarrassment. Publish a national infrastructure capacity assessment before approving any new industrial projects. Stop announcing investment wins that you can’t deliver on. Or keep building a silicon valley on a foundation of leaking pipes and overloaded grids — and watch investors move to Thailand and Indonesia instead.

Sources: SCMP | PV Magazine

item.title: Penang Undersea Tunnel Project 'Still Active' — 14 Years, Zero Construction, RM6.3B Price Tag
item.description:
Said by: Chow Kon Yeow, Penang Chief Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-14

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 14 November 2025, Chow Kon Yeow told media that the Penang Undersea Tunnel Project is ’not an abandoned project but is still active and in the technical review and design completion phase.’ The RM6.3 billion project was proposed in 2011 under the Penang Transport Master Plan (PTMP). Fourteen years later, not a single metre of tunnel has been constructed. Meanwhile, the corruption trial of former CM Lim Guan Eng over the project continues, with MACC witnesses describing it as ‘akin to a land scam.’

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A RM6.3 billion project proposed 14 years ago with zero construction is either a failed project or a phantom one. Chow’s insistence that it’s ‘still active’ strains credibility to breaking point. The project was supposed to solve Penang’s traffic crisis — which has only worsened during the 14-year wait. Meanwhile, the former CM who approved the project is on trial for corruption related to it. At what point does ‘still active’ become ‘still pretending’?

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: ‘Technical review and design completion phase’ — after 14 years. For comparison, China built the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge (the world’s longest sea crossing) in 9 years. Singapore built the Thomson-East Coast MRT line in less time. Penang can’t complete the design phase for a tunnel that was supposed to be built a decade ago. The project exists on paper to justify the PTMP’s existence and the land reclamation that was supposed to fund it. The tunnel is Schrödinger’s infrastructure — simultaneously active and non-existent.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The PTMP was criticised from the start for its opaque procurement — using request-for-proposal mechanisms instead of open tenders. The Penang South Islands reclamation was conceived as the funding mechanism for the PTMP. So the logic is: destroy the sea to fund a tunnel that hasn’t been built in 14 years. Each project justifies the other; neither delivers for the public.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Fourteen years. RM6.3 billion. Zero metres built. Former CM on trial for corruption related to the project. Current CM insists it’s ‘still active.’ At what point do you admit this project is a failure? Cancel it, audit the spending, and redirect the funds to transport solutions that can actually be built — bus rapid transit, cycling infrastructure, traffic management. Penang’s commuters need solutions, not phantom tunnels.

Sources: The Vibes

item.title: US Declares 'Victory Against China' After Securing Unlimited Minerals Pledge From Anwar
item.description:
Said by: MalaysiaNow report

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-03

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 3 November 2025, MalaysiaNow reported that the United States had “declared victory against China” after securing a pledge from Anwar Ibrahim to not restrict exports of critical minerals and rare earth elements to the US. Under the US-Malaysia ART, Malaysia committed to “refrain from banning, or imposing quotas on, exports to the US of critical minerals or rare earth elements” — a binding trade commitment, not a goodwill gesture. Malaysia also agreed to partner with American firms “to create certainty for businesses to increase production capacity,” essentially guaranteeing US access to Malaysian mineral resources in perpetuity.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Indonesia restricted nickel exports in 2020 and used the leverage to build a domestic processing industry now worth billions of dollars — attracting smelter investments, creating tens of thousands of jobs, and moving up the value chain. Malaysia just agreed to keep sending raw materials to the US. One country used its resources to industrialise. The other agreed to remain a raw material supplier. Rare earth export controls are a key geopolitical tool — perhaps the most powerful one a resource-rich country possesses. China controls 60% of global rare earth mining and 90% of processing, and uses export controls as geopolitical leverage. Malaysia gave up the same strategic tool in exchange for a tariff reduction from 25% to 19%.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: When the US “declares victory against China” using your resources, you are not a partner — you are a pawn. The framing says it all: this was about US strategic interests, and Malaysia was the tool. The long-term cost is incalculable. Critical minerals are finite. Once committed to unrestricted export, Malaysia cannot later impose controls without breaching the ART and triggering US retaliation. The country has locked itself into raw material dependency. Anwar’s defenders argue the deal preserves market access and attracts investment. But market access at the cost of resource sovereignty is a losing trade. Ask any country that exported its resources without adding value — they are all still developing countries.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s resource export history is a cautionary tale. The country was once the world’s largest tin producer — it exported raw tin for decades while others built processing industries. By the time Malaysia tried to add value, the tin market had moved on. The same pattern played out with rubber, palm oil, and petroleum. Each time, Malaysia exported raw commodities while neighbours built processing capacity. Indonesia’s nickel strategy — restrict exports, force domestic processing, capture value — is exactly what Malaysia should have done with rare earths. Instead, Anwar signed away the option.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Indonesia used resource nationalism to build a billion-dollar nickel processing industry. You used your resources to get a 6-percentage-point tariff reduction. One strategy builds industrial capacity. The other locks you into raw material dependency. Renegotiate the minerals clause. Impose a domestic processing requirement before export. Build rare earth refining capacity in Malaysia instead of shipping raw materials to American factories. Or keep letting Washington “declare victory” using your resources — and explain to the next generation why Malaysia is still a raw material supplier.

Sources: MalaysiaNow

item.title: 'Stop Lecturing Us on Sovereign Rights' — US Envoy Told Off Amid Anger Over Trade Deal
item.description:
Said by: Malaysian public and politicians

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-02

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Days after the US-Malaysia trade deal was signed, public anger boiled over. The US envoy was publicly told to “stop lecturing us on sovereign rights” amid growing fury over what critics called Anwar’s “surrender” to Washington. MalaysiaNow reported the confrontation. The deal required Malaysia to align with US export controls, sanctions, and trade enforcement — provisions widely seen as infringing on sovereignty. Adding insult to injury, details of the agreement were published on the White House website before being shared with the Malaysian Parliament, fueling perceptions that Malaysia was an afterthought in its own trade negotiations.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When your own people are telling foreign diplomats to stop lecturing them, the government has a legitimacy problem. The deal triggered a rare moment of bipartisan fury — both opposition and ruling coalition members questioned the terms. A deal signed in haste, published by the other side first, and defended after the fact is not diplomacy — it is capitulation with a press release. Malaysia’s neutral foreign policy — maintained across BN, PH 1.0, and PN — was compromised in one signing ceremony.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government’s defence was that the deal preserved market access and prevented worse tariffs. But “it could have been worse” is not a negotiating triumph — it’s an admission of weakness. Publishing the deal on the White House website before Parliament saw it was not a procedural oversight — it was a power display by Washington showing who controls the narrative. Anwar’s team either agreed to this sequencing (which is humiliating) or didn’t anticipate it (which is incompetent). Either way, the Malaysian public learned about their own trade deal from a foreign government’s website.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a long history of trade agreements that prioritise access over sovereignty. The TPPA under Najib triggered massive protests over investor-state dispute settlement (ISDS) clauses. PH 1.0 withdrew from the TPPA, calling it an infringement on sovereignty. PH 2.0 has now signed a bilateral deal with sovereignty provisions that are arguably worse — and did so without the parliamentary scrutiny that even the TPPA received. The cycle is consistent: opposition parties criticise trade deals as sovereignty surrenders, then sign similar or worse deals when they come to power.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You withdrew from the TPPA because it infringed on sovereignty. Then you signed a bilateral deal that commits Malaysia to US export controls, sanctions alignment, and unrestricted mineral exports — and let Washington publish the terms before your own Parliament saw them. If the TPPA was a sovereignty problem, this deal is worse. Table the full agreement in Parliament for debate. Publish a clause-by-clause comparison with the TPPA you rejected. Explain why this is different — if you can. Or admit that sovereignty concerns were only useful when you were in opposition.

Sources: MalaysiaNow

item.title: Chow Admits Penang's 'Rapid Development' Not Felt by Some Communities — After 16 Years of DAP Rule
item.description:
Said by: Chow Kon Yeow, Penang Chief Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-02

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 2 November 2025, Penang CM Chow Kon Yeow admitted that the state’s ‘rapid development’ was ’not felt by some communities.’ He urged DAP leaders to ‘stay close to the people and take care of the grassroots’ and said the party must focus on solving everyday problems like flooding, traffic congestion, water supply, and housing repairs. He acknowledged that improving ground-level performance would reinforce public confidence in DAP.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: This is an extraordinary admission from the leader of Malaysia’s most development-aggressive state. DAP has governed Penang since 2008 — 16 years of unbroken rule. If after 16 years, the CM is admitting that communities aren’t feeling the benefits of development, the problem isn’t the messaging — it’s the development model. Penang’s GDP has grown, FDI has flowed in, and property prices have soared. But the fishermen, the hawkers, the working class, and the rural communities have been left behind.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Chow’s admission came at a DAP internal event — it was directed at party leaders, not the public. The message was essentially: ‘we have an optics problem, fix it at the grassroots.’ But the problem isn’t optics — it’s substance. Penang’s development model prioritises FDI, reclamation, and mega-projects over affordable housing, public transport, and community infrastructure. Traffic is among the worst in Malaysia. Housing is unaffordable. Floods are worsening. The development that Chow celebrates is visible to investors; it’s invisible to residents.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Aliran’s 2025 report documented ‘systematic failures in planning’ in Penang — traffic, floods, and vanishing green spaces. Residents demanded transparency as the state approved development after development without adequate infrastructure. FMT reported DAP Youth blaming PN for traffic woes — a laughable deflection when DAP has controlled planning approvals for 16 years.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Sixteen years. That’s how long DAP has governed Penang. If your own CM admits the people aren’t feeling the development, the development model is broken. Stop building for investors and start building for residents. Affordable housing, functional public transport, flood mitigation, and green spaces should be the priority — not more reclamation projects and industrial zones that ordinary Penangites can’t access.

Sources: FMT | Aliran

item.title: Lowy Institute: 'Anwar's International Success Sours at Home' — US Trade Deal Backlash
item.description:
Said by: Lowy Institute Analysis

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In November 2025, the Lowy Institute published an analysis of the US-Malaysia trade deal (ART), concluding that “Anwar’s international success sours at home.” The analysis identified ‘poison pill’ clauses that function as a “loyalty test” requiring Malaysia to align with US strategic interests — including export controls, sanctions enforcement, and unrestricted critical mineral exports. The Lowy Institute noted that the 19% tariff on Malaysian goods was maintained, not reduced to zero as Anwar’s public framing implied. The deal was characterised as a US strategic win dressed up as a bilateral partnership.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When an independent, respected international think tank describes your trade deal as containing “poison pills” and “loyalty tests,” the government’s spin that this was a diplomatic triumph collapses. The Lowy analysis confirmed what domestic critics had been saying: the ART prioritises US interests over Malaysian sovereignty. The 19% tariff — still among the highest imposed on any US trading partner — was presented domestically as a negotiating win, when in reality it represents a failure to secure the zero-tariff access that competitors like Vietnam achieved.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar framed the trade deal as proof of Malaysia’s elevated international standing. The Lowy Institute framed it as proof that Malaysia got outmanoeuvred. The gap between the government’s narrative and independent analysis is enormous. The “poison pill” clauses — requiring alignment with US export controls and sanctions — effectively subordinate Malaysia’s foreign policy to Washington’s strategic agenda. For a PM who champions non-alignment and Global South solidarity, signing a deal that an international think tank describes as a “loyalty test” is a contradiction that cannot be spun away.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s trade deal history is littered with agreements that looked good in press conferences and bad in practice. The TPPA under Najib was shelved after public fury over sovereignty clauses. The RCEP was signed but implementation has been slow. Each agreement follows the same pattern: government celebrates the signing, analysts identify the problems, public anger builds, and the government pivots to damage control. The ART follows this pattern exactly — except this time, the critics include a respected international institution, not just domestic opposition.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The Lowy Institute called your trade deal a “loyalty test.” Independent analysts identified “poison pill” clauses. The tariff stayed at 19%, not zero. Your own narrative doesn’t match the analysis. Publish the full text of the ART with a clause-by-clause explanation of what Malaysia committed to. Commission an independent economic impact assessment. Explain why Malaysia accepted “loyalty test” provisions that other countries rejected. Or keep celebrating a deal that international analysts describe as a strategic defeat.

Sources: Lowy Institute | MalaysiaNow

item.title: Personal Bankruptcies Rise 5.7% — Self-Declared Bankruptcy Trend 'Growing Concern'
item.description:
Said by: Department of Insolvency Malaysia

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In the first nine months of 2025, Malaysia recorded 4,875 personal bankruptcy cases — a 5.7% increase from the same period in 2024. The Insolvency Department Director-General flagged a “growing concern”: the rise in self-declared bankruptcies, where Malaysians are so financially distressed they voluntarily declare themselves bankrupt rather than waiting for creditors to force the process. The data indicates that financial distress is not concentrated among business owners — it is spreading to salaried workers, young professionals, and gig economy participants.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When people voluntarily declare bankruptcy, it means they have given up hope of recovery. Self-declared bankruptcy is an act of financial surrender — it signals that the individual sees no path to repaying debts through income alone. A 5.7% rise in total cases, combined with a rising self-declared trend, suggests that Malaysia’s cost-of-living crisis is pushing ordinary households past breaking point. Official inflation is 1.3%. But bankruptcies are rising 5.7%. Those two numbers cannot both be telling the truth about the economy.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government celebrates GDP growth, FDI records, and low headline inflation while an increasing number of Malaysians are literally declaring themselves bankrupt. The disconnect between macro indicators and micro reality is the defining feature of the Madani economy: the numbers look good in Putrajaya, but the rakyat is drowning in debt. The bankruptcy threshold was raised from RM50,000 to RM100,000 in 2020 — meaning current figures actually undercount the problem, since people with debts under RM100,000 can no longer be declared bankrupt by creditors. The real financial distress is worse than the data shows.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Personal bankruptcy has been rising in Malaysia for years. Under BN, the government raised the bankruptcy threshold to mask the trend. Under PH 1.0, a moratorium on bankruptcy proceedings was introduced during COVID. Under PH 2.0, the threshold remains at RM100,000 and bankruptcy cases are climbing again. Each government has managed the optics rather than addressing the root causes: stagnant real wages, rising living costs, easy access to consumer credit, and inadequate financial literacy. The cycle continues because addressing it would require admitting that the economy is not working for ordinary people.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Bankruptcies are up 5.7% while you claim inflation is 1.3%. Self-declared bankruptcies are rising — people are so desperate they’re surrendering voluntarily. Your GDP growth is meaningless if it doesn’t translate into household financial security. Expand financial counselling services through AKPK. Regulate predatory lending practices, especially in the buy-now-pay-later sector. Publish household debt-to-income data by income quintile so the rakyat can see the real picture. Or keep quoting macro numbers while more Malaysians walk into the Insolvency Department to declare themselves bankrupt.

Sources: Bernama | Malay Mail | The Star

item.title: Rakyat Says 1.3% Inflation is a Lie — 'Don't Know Where Madanon Gets These Numbers'
item.description:
Said by: Public Sentiment / Social Media

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Malaysia’s official inflation rate for October 2025 was reported at 1.3% by the Department of Statistics (DOSM). Malaysians responded with open mockery on social media, with comments like “don’t know where Madanon gets these numbers.” The disconnect between headline inflation and lived experience is stark: food-away-from-home inflation ran at 4.3-4.7%, far exceeding the headline rate. Rental costs, healthcare, and education expenses — categories that disproportionately affect middle and lower-income households — all rose faster than the official composite figure suggests.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When citizens openly mock their government’s official statistics, it signals a collapse in institutional credibility. The 1.3% headline figure is technically accurate — it measures the CPI basket as defined by DOSM. But the CPI basket doesn’t reflect how ordinary Malaysians actually spend their money. A hawker meal that cost RM5 two years ago now costs RM7-8. Mamak teh tarik has gone from RM1.80 to RM2.50. These are the prices people feel daily — and they bear no resemblance to 1.3%. The gap between official statistics and lived reality erodes public trust in all government data.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government uses the 1.3% figure to claim economic stability while ignoring the composition of that figure. Headline CPI is dragged down by administered prices (fuel subsidies, price controls) that mask underlying inflation. When you subsidise RON95 and control flour and sugar prices, headline inflation looks low — but the moment subsidies are withdrawn, the suppressed inflation hits all at once. The government is not controlling inflation — it is hiding it behind subsidies it can’t afford to maintain. Meanwhile, the items that aren’t price-controlled — food, services, healthcare, education — are rising at 3-5x the headline rate.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has used headline inflation figures to paint a rosier picture than reality. Under Najib, the GST introduction in 2015 caused a spike that was partially masked by rebates and handouts. Under Muhyiddin, pandemic-era price controls suppressed official inflation while actual costs soared. The pattern is consistent: governments manipulate the visible number while ordinary households bear invisible cost increases. PH promised transparency and honesty in governance — but its handling of inflation data is indistinguishable from its predecessors.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The rakyat is laughing at your inflation numbers. That should worry you more than the numbers themselves. Publish a “cost of living index” that tracks the actual basket of goods and services used by B40 and M40 households — not the CPI composite that includes imported luxury goods and administered prices. Show Malaysians you understand what they’re actually paying. Or keep quoting 1.3% while mamak meals cost RM8 — and wonder why nobody believes anything you say about the economy.

Sources: DOSM | FMT | Malaysiakini

item.title: Youth Unemployment Stuck at 12.28% — One in Eight Young Malaysians Can't Find Work
item.description:
Said by: DOSM / ILO / World Bank Data

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-11-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: As of late 2025, Malaysia’s youth unemployment rate (ages 15-24) remains stuck at 12.28% — roughly 297,500 unemployed young Malaysians. This is three to four times higher than the overall national unemployment rate of 3.2%. DOSM, the ILO, and World Bank data all confirm the structural nature of the problem: Malaysia produces graduates the economy doesn’t need, while industries that are hiring can’t find workers with the right skills. The “Madani economy” that Anwar celebrates apparently doesn’t include young people.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: One in eight young Malaysians can’t find work. This isn’t a cyclical downturn — it’s a structural failure of the education-to-employment pipeline. Universities churn out graduates in fields with limited demand while industries face acute shortages in technical, digital, and skilled trades. The result: a generation of educated young Malaysians who are overqualified for available jobs and underqualified for the jobs that exist. Youth unemployment at 12.28% is a social time bomb — it drives brain drain, delays household formation, increases mental health burdens, and erodes faith in democratic institutions.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: The government touts GDP growth above 5% and record FDI as proof the economy is thriving. But an economy that grows while leaving one in eight young people unemployed is not thriving — it is extracting value without distributing opportunity. PH promised TVET reform, graduate employability programmes, and industry-aligned education. Three years later, the numbers haven’t moved. The programmes exist on paper. The graduates remain unemployed. The gap between government rhetoric (“Malaysia is booming”) and youth reality (“I can’t find a job”) is the defining economic contradiction of the Anwar era.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Youth unemployment has been structurally elevated in Malaysia for over a decade. Under BN, the rate hovered around 10-11%. Under PH 1.0, it briefly dipped before COVID. Under Muhyiddin and Ismail Sabri, it spiked to 14%+ during the pandemic. Under PH 2.0, it has settled at 12.28% — better than the pandemic peak but worse than pre-pandemic levels. No government has solved it because no government has been willing to reform the education system that produces the mismatch. Universities are political institutions in Malaysia — restructuring them means challenging entrenched interests.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: One in eight young Malaysians can’t find work. Your GDP is growing above 5%. Your FDI is at record highs. And your youth are unemployed. The disconnect is structural — and it’s your responsibility now. Reform TVET funding so it matches industry demand. Publish graduate employability data by university and programme so students can make informed choices. Partner with industries on apprenticeship programmes with guaranteed employment pathways. Or keep celebrating GDP growth while a generation of young Malaysians leaves the country to find work elsewhere.

Sources: DOSM Labour Force Survey | World Bank Malaysia Economic Monitor | ILO

item.title: Zafrul: 'We Had No Choice But to Negotiate' — The Most Honest and Damning Defence of the US Trade Deal
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-29

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Speaking in Parliament on 29 October 2025, Tengku Zafrul delivered what he intended as a defence of the ART but what critics heard as an admission of weakness: ‘We had no choice but to negotiate. Whether we like it or not, we need to defend our access to the US markets, even when under pressure.’ He called the deal the ‘best case scenario’ and accused detractors of ‘ignorance regarding the intense, behind-the-scenes negotiations.’

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: ‘We had no choice’ is the most revealing statement any PH minister has made. It confirms what critics suspected: Malaysia negotiated from a position of weakness, accepted terms it didn’t like, and signed because the alternative — US tariffs — was worse. That may be true. But ‘we had no choice’ is not a defence; it’s an epitaph for Malaysia’s negotiating leverage. When your minister tells Parliament the country had no options, you are telling every future trading partner that Malaysia will take whatever deal is offered.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Other countries had choices. Vietnam, Indonesia, and Thailand all face US tariffs but haven’t signed agreements with the same provisions. Indonesia negotiated its own terms. Vietnam leveraged its manufacturing base. Malaysia, under Zafrul, signed first and defended later. The ’no choice’ framing is particularly damaging because it removes agency — as if Malaysia is a passive recipient of geopolitical forces rather than a sovereign nation with options.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s trade negotiators have historically been among ASEAN’s most skilled. Mahathir — for all his flaws — refused to be pushed around on trade. Even Najib’s TPPA negotiations extracted significant carve-outs. Zafrul’s admission that Malaysia ‘had no choice’ represents a historic low in trade negotiation confidence.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: ‘We had no choice’ is not a reform slogan. It’s a surrender statement. If you truly had no choice, that’s a failure of diplomatic preparation, not a defence of the outcome. The rakyat deserves to know: what choices did you consider and reject? What alternatives were evaluated? Or did you just sign what Washington put in front of you?

Sources: Malay Mail | CNA

item.title: Selangor Water: 27% Lost to Leaks — Amirudin Touts Smart Meters While Pipes Burst
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari, Selangor Menteri Besar

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-27

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 27 October 2025, Amirudin told Parliament that pilot smart water meter projects in Selangor had proven effective — citing Bukit Jelutong where non-revenue water (NRW) was recorded at 27%. He urged the federal government to adopt smart meters nationwide. Separately, the Selangor government announced it would absorb increased water costs for over one million users and accelerate pipe replacement at 300km per year, targeting a 20% water reserve margin by 2030.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Selangor has been PH-governed since 2008. After 17 years, 27% of treated water is still lost to leaks and inefficiencies in a pilot area. Across the state, NRW rates are higher. Air Selangor serves 9.62 million consumers in Selangor, KL, and Putrajaya — the largest water utility in the country. The decades-long failure to fix leaking pipes while water disruptions regularly affect millions of consumers is one of PH’s most tangible governance failures.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Amirudin is lecturing the federal government about smart meters while his own state can’t keep pipes from bursting. Selangor has had repeated water crises — pollution shutdowns, supply disruptions, and rationing — under his watch. The RM320 million Sungai Selangor Alternative Raw Water scheme (SJAM) is being framed as a solution, but the fundamental problem is infrastructure decay that PH has failed to address in 17 years of state government.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: The 2023 and 2024 water disruptions in the Klang Valley affected millions and were caused by river pollution shutting down treatment plants — a problem that proper investment in alternative sources would have prevented. Amirudin’s water master plan was only announced in 2025, after 17 years of PH governance.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You’ve governed Selangor’s water infrastructure for 17 years. NRW is still at 27%. Pipes still burst. Treatment plants still shut down from pollution. And your solution is to tell the federal government to buy smart meters? Fix your own pipes first. The rakyat doesn’t need a masterplan announced in year 17 — they needed it in year 1.

Sources: NST | Yahoo News

item.title: Malaysia Committed to Never Banning Rare Earth Exports to the US — Under Zafrul's Trade Deal
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister / USTR

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Under the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade signed by Tengku Zafrul on 26 October 2025, Malaysia committed to not imposing ‘quantitative restrictions’ (bans or quotas) on exports of critical or rare-earth minerals to the United States. The USTR fact sheet explicitly highlights this as a US victory. Meanwhile, Zafrul dismissed Mahathir’s claim that Malaysia agreed to supply unprocessed rare earth at low returns, saying existing policy mandates domestic processing before export.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Rare earth minerals are the 21st century’s most strategic resource — essential for semiconductors, defence systems, EVs, and renewable energy. China controls roughly 60% of global rare earth mining and 90% of processing. The US desperately needs alternative supplies, and Malaysia — with Lynas’s Kuantan plant — is a key node. By committing in writing to never restrict exports to the US, Malaysia surrendered one of its most powerful geopolitical leverage points for nothing more than maintaining the status quo tariff.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Indonesia, Australia, and other mineral-rich nations are using export controls as negotiating leverage to attract downstream processing and technology transfer. Malaysia gave that leverage away. The commitment means that even if a future government wants to use rare earth as a bargaining chip — as China has done effectively — Malaysia is contractually bound not to. Zafrul traded a strategic asset for a tariff rate that didn’t even improve.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: China’s rare earth export restrictions have been its most effective trade weapon for decades. Indonesia’s nickel export ban forced companies to build smelters domestically, creating jobs and value-add. Malaysia had the same opportunity — and signed it away in a trade deal.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You just gave the United States a written guarantee that Malaysia will never use its rare earth resources as leverage. Not for technology transfer. Not for downstream jobs. Not for better trade terms. For nothing. Your minister says Malaysia’s processing-first policy is intact — but the agreement says you can’t restrict exports. When Washington comes knocking for raw minerals, your ‘policy’ will fold against a treaty obligation.

Sources: USTR Fact Sheet | FMT

item.title: Tengku Zafrul Negotiated the US Trade Deal Critics Call 'Worst Agreement Since Independence' — Then Got Promoted
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, former MITI Minister

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 26 October 2025, then-MITI Minister Tengku Zafrul signed the Agreement on Reciprocal Trade (ART) with the United States at the 47th ASEAN Summit in Kuala Lumpur. The deal committed Malaysia to providing preferential market access for US products across chemicals, machinery, vehicles, dairy, poultry, pork, rice, and fuel ethanol. Malaysia also committed to not imposing bans or quotas on the export of critical or rare-earth minerals to the US. In return, the US maintained a 19% reciprocal tariff on Malaysian goods — with only 60.4% of tariff lines zero-rated. Critics from across the political spectrum, including Mahathir and Rafizi Ramli, called it a one-sided surrender.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The USTR fact sheet was published on the White House website before it was shared with Malaysia’s Parliament — meaning Washington told the world what Malaysia agreed to before Malaysia’s own legislators knew. The deal requires Malaysia to mirror US sanctions, act against ‘unfair practices’ by third-country firms (read: China), and open markets in sectors where Malaysian producers are vulnerable. Eurasia Review noted the deal was ‘hastily put together to please the US president during his visit’ and was ’not intended to catch much attention.’ It caught plenty.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Zafrul defended the deal as the ‘best case scenario’ — as if negotiating from weakness is an achievement. He told Parliament: ‘We had no choice but to negotiate.’ That is not a defence; it’s a confession. Malaysia’s neutral foreign policy — maintained for decades — was compromised in one signing ceremony. Zafrul’s reward for this? A promotion to Senior Political Advisor to the PM and appointment as MIDA chairman. In PH’s Malaysia, you get promoted for signing away sovereignty.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has signed trade agreements before without this level of controversy. The CPTPP was negotiated over years with extensive parliamentary scrutiny. The ART was rushed through in weeks, signed at a summit, and defended after the fact. The pattern of PH economic decisions — from 5G dual network to ECRL to this — is consistent: make the politically convenient choice, then spend months defending it.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your minister signed a deal that commits Malaysia to US sanctions compliance and mineral export guarantees, and your defence is ‘we had no choice.’ You always have a choice — you chose to capitulate. Then you promoted the man who did it. If this was really the ‘best case scenario,’ Malaysia’s negotiating position under PH is worse than anyone imagined.

Sources: CNA | USTR | Eurasia Review

item.title: US Trade Deal Details Published on White House Website Before Malaysian Parliament Was Told
item.description:
Said by: Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz, MITI Minister / White House

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: When the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade was signed on 26 October 2025, the USTR published a detailed fact sheet on the White House website outlining Malaysia’s commitments — including preferential market access for US goods, commitments on rare earth minerals, and tariff schedules. Malaysian MPs and the public learned the details of their own country’s trade agreement from an American government website. The deal’s terms were published in Washington before they were presented in Putrajaya.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: A sovereign nation’s trade commitments should be communicated to its own parliament and citizens before they are announced by the other party. The fact that Malaysians had to read the White House website to understand what their government had agreed to is a humiliation of parliamentary sovereignty. It also meant the US controlled the narrative — the terms were framed as an American victory before Malaysia could spin its own story.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH promised transparent governance and parliamentary supremacy. Then Zafrul signed a trade agreement and let Washington publish the terms first. Fahmi Fadzil is the Communications Minister — where was the proactive Malaysian disclosure? The sequence tells you everything about the power dynamics: the US dictated the terms, dictated the timeline, and dictated the narrative. Malaysia’s role was to sign and defend.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia’s TPPA negotiations under Najib were also criticised for opacity — but at least the government attempted to control its own narrative. Under PH, the narrative was literally ceded to the White House. Eurasia Review noted the deal was ‘hastily put together’ and ’not intended to catch much attention.’ The haste is the point — rushed deals favour the stronger party.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You let the White House tell Malaysians what their own government agreed to. That is not a partnership of equals. That is a relationship where one party writes the press release and the other party nods. If you can’t even control the communications around your own trade deals, how do you expect Malaysians to believe you controlled the negotiations?

Sources: USTR Fact Sheet | Eurasia Review

item.title: US-Malaysia Trade Deal Called 'Worst Agreement Since Independence' and 'Act of Surrender'
item.description:
Said by: Tommy Thomas, Former Attorney General (and critics)

Category: Economy

Date: 2025-10-26

The Deal That Sold Malaysia’s Sovereignty

On 26 October 2025, Anwar Ibrahim and Donald Trump signed the US-Malaysia Agreement on Reciprocal Trade (ART) at the ASEAN Summit. Former Attorney-General Tommy Thomas called it the worst agreement Malaysia has entered since independence in 1957. He was not exaggerating.

The Facts

Fact: The deal maintains a 19% US tariff on Malaysian goods — down from 25%, but still punitive by any standard. Malaysia’s effective tariff on US goods before this deal was roughly 6%. The US got the better end by a factor of three.

Fact: Malaysia committed to: enforce US tariff evasion rules against third countries, align with US export controls and sanctions regimes, not ban or restrict exports of critical minerals or rare earths to the US, refrain from imposing digital services taxes on US tech companies, and purchase additional American goods and services.

Fact: A termination clause allows the US to reimpose the original 25% tariff if Malaysia enters a trade deal that “jeopardizes essential US interests.” This effectively gives Washington veto power over Malaysia’s independent trade policy — including with China, Malaysia’s largest trading partner.

Fact: The deal’s full text was first published on the White House website before being shared with Malaysian Parliament. Malaysian lawmakers learned the terms of their own trade agreement from a foreign government’s press release.

Fact: Former PM Mahathir said the provisions amounted to “handing over” the country’s independence. Even for a man who oversaw his own share of questionable deals, this one was a bridge too far.

Fact: East Asia Forum’s analysis, titled “The Art of the Deal Lost in US-Malaysia Pact,” argued Malaysia surrendered critical mineral export controls, digital services taxation rights, and independent trade policy in exchange for a tariff cut that still left Malaysia paying three times what the US pays.

Fact: The Guardian reported that Anwar “defended” the deal by arguing it was “the best Malaysia could get under the circumstances” — an argument that concedes the deal is bad while claiming it could have been worse.

Fact: SCMP reported Anwar “slammed” critics for accusing him of surrendering sovereignty — but never addressed the substance of their arguments, only their tone.

Why It Matters

  • This is not a reciprocal deal by any definition of the word. Malaysia makes extensive, binding concessions across minerals, digital taxation, sanctions alignment, and procurement. The US reduces a punitive tariff by 6 percentage points and retains the right to reimpose it at will. In any negotiation textbook, this is called capitulation.
  • The sovereignty clause is the most dangerous provision. If Malaysia wants to deepen trade ties with China — its largest trading partner — the US can terminate the ART and reimpose 25% tariffs. Malaysia has effectively given Washington a veto over its foreign economic policy.
  • Publishing the details on the White House website before informing Parliament is not just embarrassing — it is a constitutional affront. Trade agreements that constrain national sovereignty should require parliamentary debate and approval before signing. Anwar bypassed the legislature on the most consequential economic agreement in decades.
  • Every ASEAN neighbour is watching. Indonesia walked away from a similar US proposal. Vietnam negotiated harder. Thailand delayed. Malaysia signed first and asked questions later. This weakens Malaysia’s negotiating position in every future bilateral and multilateral trade discussion.
  • The “best we could get” defence is an admission of weakness, not a justification. If this was truly the best outcome, it means Malaysia’s diplomatic leverage is weaker than the government has claimed — which raises questions about the entire Madani foreign policy brand. Sources: The Guardian · East Asia Forum · SCMP · Straits Times
